The boundary between the diencephalon and the mesencephalon.
Embryonic structure that gives rise to the forebrain midbrain boundary.
Pancreatic bud that develops from a ridge on the ventral side of the intestinal bulb primordium.
Pancreatic bud that forms dorsal and adjacent to the developing intestine.

comments: Furthermore, zebrafish exocrine progenitors appear to arise from endoderm rostral to the gut tube, which it subsequently joins, rather than as an extension of the epithelium of the main duct. Thus, we argue against a classical model (Field et al., 2003) in which the zebrafish exocrine pancreas buds from either a rod of pre-intestinal endoderm or the gut tube which in zebrafish gives rise to the intestine (Wallace and Pack, 2003).  ZDB-PUB-050623-3.

definition.val: System responsible for the production and movement of pancreatic hormones and enzymes.
Duct that collects and carries secretions of the exocrine pancreas.
Cells that initially are located adjacent to the spinal cord and notochord that migrate from their initial medial position radially through the myotome to the lateral surface of the somites.
Myocardium of the heart atrium.
A heart valve located between the ventricle and bulbus arteriosus.
Presumptive structure at the animal poll of the blastula that will develop into ectoderm via the epiblast. Determined by fate mapping.
Presumptive structure fated to become mesoderm. Determined by fate mapping.
Region of the hypoblast that is located along the rostral-caudal axis of the embryo.

Hypohyals are the most anterior of the bilaterally paired ventral hyoid arch elements. The two hypohyals in zebrafish ossify at the anterodorsal and anteroventral tips of the ceratohyal cartilages; no separate hypohyal cartilages are present.

Epibranchials 1-3 in adults are compound elements formed by the fusion of epibranchial cartilages and the so-called uncinate processes.  Posteromedially directed uncinate processes develop on the posterior edge of epibranchials 1-3. These appear as separate centers of chondrification (6.2 mm) that later fuse laterally to the posterior edges of the epibranchial cartilages (6.8 mm).
Surface structure that develops into a paired fin.
Fin bud that develops into the pelvic fin.

nodes.lbl: apical ectodermal ridge pelvic fin bud

definition.val: Apical ectodermal ridge that is part of the pelvic fin bud.

synonyms.val: apical ectodermal ridge pelvic fin buds

definition.val: Mesoderm part of pelvic fin bud.
Skeleton that supports the pelvic fin.  The skeleton consists of consists of supporting endochondral radials and dermal fin rays or lepidotrichia.
Epithelium of the regenerating fin that is formed as part of the healing process.
Embryonic structure that develops from the pancreas primordium and develops into the pancreas.  At Prim-5 the buds are located between the dorsal aorta and the posterior cardinal vein.
The anterior lateral line ganglia develops from cranial ectodermal placodes and contain sensory neurons that innervate the anterior lateral line system.
Lateral projections from the first centrum in cyprinids, absent in catostomids, and absent in characiphysans (Fink and Fink, 1996).
Lateral projection from the second centrum, absent in Siluriformes (Fink and Fink, 1996).
Neural arch 3 is expanded and fused to supraneural 3 as part of a neural complex roofing and protecting the neural canal.
Neural arch 4 is expanded and fused to supraneural 3 as part of the neural complex. A reduced neural spine projects from the posterior margin of the neural complex.
The parapophysis + rib, fused to the centrum, extends laterally then angles anteroventrally. A thin plate, the os suspensorium fuses to its median surface.
Posterior to the Weberian apparatus, otophysan supraneurals form as median, unpaired, rod- or platelike elements, as in other actinopterygians. Supraneural 4 has been lost in zebrafish.

Cartilage that is the dorsal component of the first pharyngeal arch.  It is roughly triangular with slightly concave dorsal edges and convex ventral edges.  The anteroventral corner gives rise the quadrate and articulates with the posterior end of  Meckel's cartilage.  The anterodorsal tip articulates with the lateral edge of the ethmoid plate and the posteroventral edge articulates with the anterodorsal edge of the hyosymplectic.
Pharyngeal arch cartilage that is bilaterally paired and the largest cartilaginous component of the ventral hyoid arch.

Dermal bone that first appears as paired ossifications in the membrane (dermis) dorsolateral to the ethmoid cartilage and anteriolateral to the lateral ethmoids, later having the appearance of one butterfly-shaped element. Fused to the anterodorsal margin of the mesethmoid. Bordered anteriorly by the kinethmoid and posteriorly by the frontal.
Small rounded to egg-shaped cartilage bone that sits in an anteriolateral depression on the prevomer. Articulates with the autopalatine in all cypriniforms.

Bilaterally paired neurocranium cartilage which is initially plate-like and formed from the anterior fusion between the bilaterally paired trabecula cranii. It grows dorsally between the laminae orbitonasales to attain a complex shape.

Small median unpaired dermal sesamoid bone in the premaxillary-ethmoid ligament between the mesethmoid and the premaxilla that enhances the protractability of the upper jaw. It forms in the premaxillary-ethmoid ligament. The kinethmoid arose de novo in the ancestor of Cypriniformes, the only group within Ostariophysi, in which it is found (Fink and Fink, 1981).
Plate like dermal bone, variable in size, though usually one of the smallest in the series. Bears 2-3 neuromast pores enclosed within the infraorbital canal (Nelson, 1969).

Plate like dermal bone, variable in size, but usually largest in the series. Usually fused with IO4 (eg. Opsariichthys, Nelson (1969)). Usually bears 3 neuromast pores enclosed within the infraorbital canal (Nelson, 1969).
Plate like dermal bone. Usually fused with infraorbital 3.
Anatomical cluster that is a anteriolateral part of the cranium and structurally supports the eye.

A pair of small dish-shaped bones that cap the anterior and posterior margin of the eyeball. The sclerotic bones also called scleral ossicles form from the scleral cartilage between the sclera and the optic choroid.  The ossicles develop via unilateral perichondral ossification of the scleral cartilage from two centers opposite one another in the eye.

Cartilage bone that contacts the pterotic anteriorly, the supraoccipital and parietal dorsally, and the exoccipital ventrally. The epiotic enclose the posterior semicircular canal of the inner ear and contribute to the hind margin of the cranium.
Anatomical cluster that is located in the posterior region of the cranium and forms the margin of the foramen magnum and occipital condyle.
Second radial of the pelvic fin.
Third radial of the pelvic fin.
First radial of the pelvic fin.

Lepidotrichium which is part of the dorsal fin.  Bony, bilaterally paired segmented dermal ossification extending distally from the endoskeletal supports.  They develop around actinotrichia as part of the dermal exoskeleton.
A range of seven to nine rod-like internal median skeletal support structures of the dorsal fin, comprised of both bone and cartilage and found between vertebrae 11 and 18.
A range of twelve to fourteen rod-like internal median skeletal support structures of the anal fin, comprised of both bone and cartilage and found between vertebrae 14 and 21.
Lepidotrichium which is part of the anal fin.
Bilaterally paired large cartilage that articulates with the chondrocranium dorsally, the opercle posteriorly, and the palatoquadrate cartilage anteriorly.
Rod-shaped paired cartilages on either side of the notochord. They unite, forming the base of the chondrocranium and are replaced by the prootic bones.
Anatomical cluster that is part of the cranium and composed of cartilage and cartilage replacement bones.
Nares that form at 40-44dpf that open to the posterior region of the olfactory pit allowing water to exit the pit.
Nares that form at 40-44dpf that open to the anterior region of the olfactory pit allowing water to enter the pit.
The highly folded olfactory epithelium.

comments:
Olfactory epithelium may be part of olfactory rosette. 
The epithelium exists before becoming folded into the rosette.

definition.val: Ventral hindbrain nuclei just caudal to the interpeduncular nucleus.
A duct connecting the esophagus to the posterior chamber of the swim bladder.
Sclerotic bone that is located anterior to the eye.
Sclerotic bone that is located posterior to the eye.
Floor plate that is part of the neural tube.
Portion of tissue on the side of the lumen of the neural tube.
Roof plate that is part of the neural tube.

The constriction between the anterior and posterior chambers of the swim bladder.  There does not appear to be an identifiable sphincter in the ductus, and therefore other musculature may regulate its opening.
Anatomical space, part of the trunk that contains the pericardial and pleuroperitoneal cavities.
Anatomical group that has as its parts distinct anatomical structures interconnected by anatomical structures at a lower level of granularity.
The interface between the dorsal neuroepithelium and the roof plate of the fourth ventricle. The source of a number of migratory neuron populations of the hindbrain.
Posterior portion of the rhombic lip contained within rhombomeres 2-8.  Gives rise to several brainstem nuclei and choroid plexus.
Anterior-most region of dorsal hindbrain within rhombomere 1, adjacent the midbrain-hindbrain boundary.

A tuft of capillaries that project into the ventricles of the brain and secretes cerebral spinal fluid. The choroid plexus is covered by a cuboidal epithelium which maintains the integrity of the blood-brain barrier.
Choroid plexus that is part of the third ventricle.
Choroid plexus that is part of the tectal ventricle.
Choroid plexus that is part of the fourth ventricle.
Choroid plexus that is part of the telencephalic ventricle.
Glands that produce calcitonin.  In adult fish the glands lie as two groups of follicles at the transverse septum close to the sinus venosus.
Mesenchyme that is part of the pelvic fin.
Apical ectodermal ridge that is part of the pelvic fin.

nodes.lbl: zone of polarizing activity pectoral fin bud

definition.val: A small group of mesodermal cells located at the posterior margin of the developing fin bud. The ZPA is thought to produce retinoic acid and  thereby provides positional information to the developing bud.

nodes.lbl: zone of polarizing activity pelvic fin bud

definition.val: A small group of mesodermal cells located at the posterior margin of the developing fin bud.  The ZPA is thought to produce retinoic acid and thereby provides positional information to the developing bud.
Embryonic region that develops into the pectoral fin bud.
Portion of tissue in the pelvic region that develops into the pelvic fin bud.
The cartilage from which both the scapula and coracoid develop.

A plate-like chondrogenic condensation within the pectoral fin, which subdivides into individual radials by decomposition of extracellular matrix in the inter-radial mesenchyme (Grandel and Schulte-Merker, 1998).
Cartilage which is part of the axial skeleton.
Cartilage which is part of the cranium.
Cartilage which is part of the pelvic fin.
Cartilage that is part of the chondrocranium.
Anatomical surface separating somites.
Surface structure composed of an array of melanophores arranged in a stripe.  Stripes are oriented from rostral to caudal.
The layer within the retina where the photoreceptor cell bodies reside.
Layer of the retina composed of the inner segments of the photoreceptor cells.

Layer of the retina composed of the outer segments of the photoreceptor cells. The photoreceptor outer segment is a specialized primary cilium consisting of a microtubule-based axoneme and regular stacks of membrane folds or disks containing the photopigments (opsins) required for the phototransduction cascade.

The anterior lateral line system develops from cranial ectodermal placodes, situated between the eye and the ear, that give rise to both the neuromasts and the anterior lateral line sensory nerves that innervate the neuromasts. The anterior lateral line system consists of small sensory patches (neuromasts) located superficially on the skin or just under the skin in fluid-filled canals on the head of all fishes and most amphibians and are innervated by several lateral line nerves, which project to the hindbrain. The anterior lateral line system is stimulated by local water displacements and vibrations, and detects propulsion of the fish through the water, as well as facilitating shoaling, prey capture, and predator and obstacle avoidance.

The lateral line consists of small sensory patches (neuromasts) located superficially on the skin or just under the skin in fluid-filled canals on the head and body of all fishes and most amphibians. The lateral line develops from cranial ectodermal placodes situated behind the ear and between the eye and ear.

The anterior lateral line consists of small sensory patches (neuromasts) located superficially on the skin or just under the skin in fluid-filled canals on the head of all fishes and most amphibians. The anterior lateral line develops from cranial ectodermal placodes situated between the eye and ear.

The posterior lateral line system develops from cranial ectodermal placodes, situated behind the ear, that give rise to both the neuromasts and the posterior lateral line sensory nerves that innervate the neuromasts. The posterior lateral line system consists of small sensory patches (neuromasts) located superficially on the skin or just under the skin in fluid-filled canals on the head of all fishes and most amphibians. The neuromasts are innervated by several lateral line nerves, which project primarily to the hindbrain. The posterior mechanosensory lateral line system is stimulated by local water displacements and vibrations, and detects propulsion of the fish through the water, as well as facilitating shoaling, prey capture, and predator and obstacle avoidance.
Neuromast that is part of the anterior lateral line. (Also see Anatomical Atlas entry for lateral line by T. Whitfield.)
Cells (completely cleaved), located underneath the superficial blastomeres and internal to the marginal blastomeres.
Anatomical structure, that consists of similar cells and intercellular matrix, aggregated according to genetically determined spatial relationships.
Anatomical group that has its parts adjacent to one another.
Cranial nerves that carry afferent and efferent fibers for the lateral line system.
Lateral line nerve that originates within the dorsal anterior lateral line ganglion and innervates the otic, supraorbital, and infraorbital neuromasts.
Lateral line nerve that originates within the ventral anterior lateral line ganglion and innervates the mandibular and opercular neuromasts.
Nerve fibers originating from the middle lateral line ganglion.  These fibers innervate the middle lateral line.
The middle lateral line ganglion develops from a cranial ectodermal placode and contains sensory neurons that innervate the middle lateral line.
Cells (completely cleaved), located at the surface of the blastodisc, above the deep blastomeres.

Acellular anatomical structure that consists of a thin sheet of fibrous proteins that underlie and support the cells of an epithelium. It separates the cells of an epithelium from any underlying tissue.
Portion of tissue, that consists of one or more layers of epithelial cells connected to each other by cell junctions and which is underlain by a basal lamina.

Material anatomical entity in a gaseous, liquid, semisolid or solid state; produced by anatomical structures or derived from inhaled and ingested substances that have been modified by anatomical structures as they pass through the body.

Anatomical structure that has as its parts two or more portions of tissue of at least two different types and which through specific morphogenetic processes forms a single distinct structural unit demarcated by bona-fide boundaries from other distinct structural units of different types.
Multi-tissue structure that is part of a compound organ.
Compound organ that contains one or more macroscopic anatomical spaces.
Compound organ that does not contain macroscopic anatomical spaces.
Multi-tissue structure that is not part of a compound organ.
Epithelium that consists of epithelial cells not arranged in one or more layers.
Epithelium that consists of more than one layer of epithelial cells.
Epithelium that consists of a single layer of epithelial cells.
Unilaminar epithelium that consists of a single layer of columnar cells.
Unilaminar epithelium that consists of a single layer of cuboidal cells.
Unilaminar epithelium that consists of a single layer of squamous cells.
Simple columnar epithelium that lines the esophagus.
Bilaterally paired cartilage encasing the semicircular canals of the inner ear.
Skeletal element that is composed of cartilage tissue and may be permanent or transient.

Bilaterally paired medial bar of cartilage formed at midorbit through medial chondrification from triangles of cartilage that later form the junction of the taeniae marginales anterior, taeniae marginales posterior and the epiphyseal bar.

Cranial cartilage which is bilaterally paired and develops as a dorsal plate-like projection of the anteriolateral ethmoid plate, which are oriented perpendicular to the ethmoid plate. It fuses dorsally to the taenia marginalis anterior, and it joins the ethmoid cartilage medially.

Cranial cartilage which is a paired anterodorsal projection of the posterior end of the parachordal cartilage. It lies lateral to the notochord, just anterior to the posterior tip of the otic capsule. The bilaterally paired occipital arch cartilages fuse anterodorsally to the posterior edges of the auditory capsules and meet medially as the tectum synoticum (by 12 dpf).
Cartilage bar (bilaterally paired) that grows anteroventrally from the epiphyseal bar to join the anteriolateral edge of the ethmoid plate.
Unpaired thin dorsal sheet of cartilage that partially covers the posterior chondrocranium.

Paired thin medially aligned bar of cartilage that extends from the anterior end of the chondrocranium to just anterior to the anterior end of the notochord. The posterior ends of the trabeculae cranii are continuous with the parachordal cartilages, and they enclose the hypophyseal fenestra.
Cartilage formed via medial fusion between bilaterally paired trabecula cranii.

comments: End stage should be adult but because of end stage of early palate changing to "unknown".

definition.val: Bilaterally paired thin continuous band of cartilage that sagitally encircles the eye.  It lies between t lies between the sclera and the optic choroid.

Pharyngeal arch cartilage that is median and is the anterior-most cartilage of the ventral hyoid arch. It chondrifies between the anterior tips of the ceratohyal cartilages, anterior to the first copula.

The interhyal is a small rod-like element that connects the ventral and dorsal parts of the hyoid arch. It articulates laterally with the posterior end of the epihyal and medially at the cartilaginous junction between the hyomandibula and the symplectic. It remains cartilaginous in adult zebrafish and other cypriniforms.

Anatomical structure consisting of at least two non-overlapping organs, multi-tissue aggregates or portion of tissues or cells of different types that does not constitute an organism, organ, multi-tissue aggregate, or portion of tissue.
Epithelial sheet on the external surface of the somite that gives rise to trunk, muscle and dermis.
Skeletal element that is composed of bone tissue.

Cartilage bar (bilaterally paired) that is first visible as an anterior extension of the auditory capsule. It grows anteriorly toward the middle of the orbit where it is connected to its mate on the other side by the epiphyseal bar.
Pharyngeal arch cartilage that forms the largest skeletal element of the ventral branchial arches.
Ceratobranchial cartilage that is bilaterally paired and articulates laterally with epibranchial 2 cartilage or bone.
Ceratobranchial cartilage that is bilaterally paired and articulates laterally with epibranchial 3 cartilage or bone.
Ceratobranchial cartilage that is bilaterally paired and articulates laterally with epibranchial 4 cartilage or bone.
Ceratobranchial cartilage that forms between hypobranchial 1 and epibranchial 1.
Ceratobranchial cartilage that is the most posterior, bilaterally paired ventral pharyngeal arch cartilage.
Small rod-like cartilage that forms between the median copulae and the ceratobranchial cartilages.

Small cartilage that forms at the anteromedial tip of ceratobranchial 4; these are not obviously present in mature specimens, due either to resorption or fusion to the cartilaginous anterior tip of ceratobranchial 4.
Small cartilage that forms at the anteromedial tip of ceratobranchial 1, lateral to copula 1.
Small cartilage that forms at the anteromedial tip of ceratobranchial 2, lateral to copula 1.
Small cartilage that forms at the anteromedial tip of ceratobranchial 3, lateral to copula 1.
Pharyngeal arch cartilage that is bilaterally paired small rod-like cartilage which articulates laterally with ceratobranchial cartilage or bone.
Epibranchial cartilage that is bilaterally paired and articulates laterally with ceratobranchial 1 cartilage or bone and the medial end of pharyngobranchial 1.
Epibranchial cartilage that is bilaterally paired and articulates laterally with ceratobranchial 3 cartilage or bone and the medial end of pharyngobranchial 3.
Epibranchial cartilage that is bilaterally paired and articulates laterally with ceratobranchial 2 cartilage or bone and the medial end of pharyngobranchial 2.
Epibranchial cartilage that is bilaterally paired and articulates laterally with ceratobranchial 4 cartilage or bone and the medial end of pharyngobranchial 4.
Paired cartilages that project medially from the medial tips of epibranchials.
Paired elongate cartilages that lie posteromedial to epibranchial 2 and medial to epibranchial 3.
Paired disc-like cartilage lying medial to and between the medial tips of epibranchials 1 and 2.
Cartilage that develops initially on the medial side of the coraco-scapular cartilage.
Small cartilage that forms at the posterior edge of the basipterygium.

Bilaterally paired small, rodlike cartilages on the ventral surface of the abdomen (present at 7.9 mm). Their posterior tips expand laterally, forming L-shaped cartilages. Medially, a posterior spur of cartilage develops, which will later ossify as the ischiac process (Weitzman 1962).
Small cartilage that forms at the posterior and medial edge of the basipterygium.
Small cartilage with a long posterior process and a shorter lateral one that forms at the posterior edge of the basipterygium, between radial cartilages 1 and 2.
Small cartilage that forms at the posterior and lateral edge of the basipterygium.
Cartilage which is part of one of the paired fins.

comments: Zebrafish don't have a stomach.

definition.val: Lepidotrichium which is part of the caudal fin.

Lepidotrichium which is part of the pectoral fin, forming the pectoral fin skeleton and articulating with the pectoral radials.  A pectoral lepidotrichium is composed of two hemitrichia that surround bundles of actinotrichia distally.
Lepidotrichium which is part of the pelvic fin.

Dermal bone that is rod-like, formed and usually contained in a fin membrane, and jointed to or adjacent to distal radials. They are bilaterally paired.  Lepidotrichia are formed from paired hemitrichia surrounding actinotrichia. There are usually two more lepidotrichia than distal radials.
Cranial ganglion which develops from an epibranchial placode.
Trunk ganglion which is part of a bilaterally paired set of sympathetic ganglia located anterior and lateral to the spinal cord.
Paired highly vascularized structures of the pronephros which are fused at the midline.  Responsible for blood filtration and begins to function between 40 and 48hpf.

Bilaterally paired tubes connecting the pronephric glomeruli to the pronephric ducts. In zebrafish and other teleosts, the pronephric nephron and tubules form after the pronephric duct, connecting to the anterior-most tip of the pronephric ducts.
Ventral most hypural of the caudal fin. Supports the lower lobe of the caudal fin.
Hypural of the caudal fin dorsal to hypural 1. Supports the lower lobe of the caudal fin.
Hypural dorsal to hypural 2 that supports the upper lobe of the caudal fin.
Hypural dorsal to hypural 3 that supports the upper lobe of the caudal fin.
Dorsal-most hypural, that supports the upper lobe of the caudal fin.
Oocyte part of stage II ovarian follicle.  The stage II oocytes are characterized by the presence of cortical alveoli, a tripartite zona radiata (vitelline envelope), and a central germinal vesicle.

comments: In stage II (cortical alveolus stage), oocytes are distinguished by the appearance of variably sized cortical alveoli and the vitelline envelope becomes prominent, ZDB-PUB-961014-1020.

definition.val: Oocyte part of stage III ovarian follicle.  Stage III oocytes contain internal vitellus droplets and peripheral yolk vesicles.

comments: In stage III (vitellogenesis), yolk proteins appear in oocytes and yolk bodies with crystalline yolk accrue during this major growth stage. Oocytes develop the capacity to respond in vitro to the steroid 17, 20-dihydroxy-4-pregnen-3-one (DHP) by undergoing oocyte maturation  ZDB-PUB-961014-1020.

definition.val: Oocyte part of stage I ovarian follicle. Stage I oocytes are basophilic, and have relatively large nuclei.

comments: Oocytes reside in nests with other oocytes (Stage IA) and then within a definitive follicle (Stage IB), where they greatly increase in size ZDB-PUB-961014-1020.

definition.val: Oocyte part of stage IV ovarian follicle. Stage IV oocytes are large and filled with noncrystalline yolky bodies.

comments: In stage IV (oocyte maturation), oocytes increase slightly in size, become translucent, and their yolk becomes non-crystalline as they undergo final meiotic maturation in vivo (and in response to DHP in vitro).  ZDB-PUB-961014-1020.

definition.val: Oocyte part of stage V ovarian follicle.

comments: In stage V (mature egg), eggs (approx. 0.75 mm) are ovulated into the ovarian lumen and are capable of fertilization  ZDB-PUB-961014-1020.

definition.val: A female gamete where meiosis has progressed to metaphase II and is able to participate in fertilization.  In zebrafish specified to be outside the body.

comments: Outside the body.

definition.val: A follicle that has undergone ovulation and no longer contains an oocyte.
Trunk ganglion which is bilaterally paired and located at the anterior end of the sympathetic ganglion chain.
Ganglion which is located in the trunk.

Nervous system that innervates smooth muscle, cardiac muscle and glands.  Regulates involuntary process such as blood circulation and digestion.  Has subdivisions sympathetic nervous system and parasympathetic nervous system.
Autonomic nervous system where the cell bodies lie in the brain and sacral region of the spinal cord.   The neurons are mainly cholinergic.

Autonomic nervous system that originates beginning at the thoracic and lumbar areas of the spinal cord.  Sympathetic neurons are primarily adrenergic producing the neurotransmitter noradrenalin along with other neuropeptides.
Process of the premaxilla that extends posteriorly and overlays the mesethmoid.

In cypriniforms the anterior portion of the palatine has a dorsomedial process which abuts against the mesethmoid. Such a process is not present in other ostariophysans or in other primitive teleosts.
Ural vertebra that is the second vertebra posterior to preural centrum 1.
Anatomical cluster that is the regional skeletal system surrounding the brain.
A vertebral centrum that is located immediately posterior to ural centrum 1. Ural centrum 2 is an unpaired median bone.
Precaudal vertebra that is not a Weberian vertebra.  Vertebra 5 through 14.
Vertebra which is the fused centra of preural 1 and ural 1. These elements do not develop independently in zebrafish.
Caudal fin lepidotrichium that is located anterior to the principal caudal fin rays. Caudal procurrent rays are unbranched.

Caudal fin lepidotrichium that is adjacent to a branched ray. Caudal principal rays may be segmented but unbranched and the main element forming the dorsal or ventral leading margin of the caudal fin or a ray that may be only segmented and branched and forms part of the middle-posterior region of the fin. A caudal principal ray is an unpaired median bone.  There are 19 principal caudal rays, with 10 in the dorsal lobe and nine in the ventral lobe. The dorsal lobe rays articulate with hypurals 3-5.
Radial which develops from the pectoral fin endoskeletal disc, supports the pectoral lepidotrichia or fin rays and connects the lepidotrichia with the primary pectoral girdle.

Pectoral fin radial which develops from the pectoral fin endoskeletal disc, supports a few pectoral lepidotrichia or fin rays and connects the lepidotrichia and distal radials with the primary pectoral girdle (scapula and coracoid).
Pectoral fin radial, develops from the pectoral fin endoskeletal disc, and is located at the proximal region of a lepidotrichium, between its hemitrichia. Pectoral fin distal radial is paired.

The propterygium is the lateralmost or anteriormost basal cartilage that originates from the pectoral fin endoskeletal disc. The bases of the pectoral lepidotrichium 1 plus the propterygium articulates with the scapula.
Intramembranous bone that forms superficially in the organism.  Dermal bones develop from the direct differentiation of mesenchymal cells into osteoblasts.
Replacement bone that forms within cartilage.

Bone that is bilaterally paired endochondral bone with extensions of membrane bone forming part of a dorsolateral protective wall around the neural canal between the back of the skull and the beginning of the neural arch series. The first Weberian ossicle. Most recently, de Pinna and Grande (2003) have proposed the claustrum is homologous to the accessory neural arch of lower teleosts.
An endochondral bone that is the ventral, posteriorly-oriented bony extension of the quadrate.
A dermal bone that is the lower, horizontal portion of the preopercle bone.
A dermal bone that is the upper, vertical portion of the preopercle bone.
Anatomical cluster which consists of two or more adjacent bones or cartilages, which may be interconnected by various types of tissue.
Endochondral bone which is the posteroventral region of the exoccipital bone.
Endochondral bone which is the posterodorsal region of the basioccipital bone.
Neural spine that is located on the fourth vertebra.
Anterior branch of the intercalarium.  The manubrium develops as a simple antero-lateral membrane bone extension from basidorsal 2.

Bony process that develops as a dorsally  projecting cartilaginous extension from the basidorsal.  The ascending process is longer relative to the manubrium in larvae (5.1-11.0 mm). By 13.0 mm, the ascending process appears highly reduced in size.
Portion of the intercalarium that  ossifies from the cartilage condensation and articulates with a groove in centrum 2.
Surface structure that is the dorsal portion of the caudal fin.
Surface structure that is the ventral portion of the caudal fin.

The second pharyngeal arch, also known as the hyoid arch, forms jaw support structures.  Anteriorly positioned cranial neural crest cells, which align with rhombomeres 4/5, contribute to the hyoid arch structures including the ceratohyal and hyosymplectic cartilages.

The first pharyngeal arch, also known as the mandibular arch, forms the jaw. The first pharyngeal arch gives rise to elements of the jaw, with the most anteriorly positioned cranial neural crest cells contributing to mandibular arch structures, including Meckel's and palatoquadrate cartilages that constitute the lower and upper jaw respectively.
Endocardium that is part of the atrium.
Endocardium that is part of the cardiac ventricle.
Endocardium that is part of the atrioventricular canal.

Portion of organism substance composed of calcium carbonate located in the otolith organ and positioned over a sensory macula; two form in each vesicle during the late segmentation period. (Also see Anatomical Atlas entry for otolith by T. Whitfield.)
Fiber layer adjacent the retinal ganglion cell layer which is comprised of their axons as they traverse to the optic nerve.
Most anterior portion of the pronephric duct (or tubule as described by Wingert et al, 2007).
Portion of the pronephric duct (or tubule as described by Wingert et al, 2007) which is just posterior to the proximal convoluted tubule.
Portion of the pronephric duct (or tubule as described by Wingert et al, 2007) which is just posterior to the proximal straight tubule.
Most posterior portion of the pronephric duct (or tubule as described by Wingert et al, 2007) which ends at the cloacal chamber at the pronephric duct opening.
Portion of the pronephric duct (or tubule as described by Wingert et al, 2007) which is just posterior to the distal early tubule.
Portion of cranial nerve II where large fascicles of axons from the optic fiber layer come together prior to leaving the eye.
Portion of organism substance that is a clear fluid that occupies the subarachnoid space and the ventricular system around and inside the brain.
Anatomical cluster that consists of a bulla formed by the exoccipital and basioccipital bones, and surrounds the lagena.
Bone that forms as a replacement of another structural tissue.
Replacement bone that forms within the connective tissue surrounding the notochord.
Replacement bone that forms within the connective tissue surrounding cartilage.
Perichordal bone that surrounds the anterior tip of the notochord.
Portion of tissue that consists of mesodermally derived cells and intercellular matrix comprised of protein fibers and carbohydrates, which supports, ensheathes and binds together other tissues.
Portion of connective tissue which surrounds the notochord.
Portion of connective tissue which surrounds cartilage.
Bone that forms directly within mesenchyme, and does not replace other tissues.
Intramembranous bone that forms deep in the organism.
Portion of connective tissue that is an extension of a bone.
Simple squamous epithelium which lines blood and lymphatic vessels and the heart.
Ventral bend in the brain occurring caudal to the adenohypophyseal placode.
Neural arch on the urostyle. It does not support a neural spine. Sanger and McCune 2002.
Anatomical cluster consisting of adjacent teeth constituting a row.

Non-material anatomical entity of three dimensions, that is generated by morphogenetic or other physiologic processes; is surrounded by one or more anatomical structures; contains one or more organism substances or anatomical structures.

comments: Part_of parents of these terms should be changed to be located_in rather then part_of when we get the appropriate relation.

definition.val: Cartilage of the lower jaw that give rise to a portion of the anguloarticular compound bone.

Dermal bone that is located posterior to the dentary and lateral to the articular and retroarticular bones of the lower jaw. The angular forms the outer dermal sheet of the posterior part of the lower jaw. The angular bears a portion of the mandibular sensory canal. The angular is a paired bone.
Internal skeletal supports of the anal fin.
Internal skeletal supports of the dorsal fin.
Neural crest that migrates into the pharyngeal arches and heart, forming connective tissue that separates the vessels of the heart.
Anatomical cluster that is the series of dermal bones associated with the infraorbital sensory canal located under and posterior to the eye.

Head muscle that originates on the anterior region of the cleithrum and inserts on the urohyal. The hypobranchial forms initially as left and right parts that later fuse together forming a cone shape.  The muscle is innervated by the occipitospinals and mainly composed of fast twitch muscle fibers.
Muscle which is part of the head.
Muscle which is paired and runs anterio-posteriorly along the heart and is covered with epicardium.
Anatomical space which is located beneath the operculum and external to the gills.
Anatomical space in which the heart is located and is surrounded by the pericardium.

Anatomical space which contains the abdominal viscera and swim bladder. Located posterior to the pericardial cavity, separated by the transverse septum. From Functional Anatomy of the Vertebrates, third edition.
CNS tract/commissure which extends between the telencephalon and diencephalon and lies along the dorsal edge of the optic stalk.
Nucleus located within the hindbrain.
Brain nucleus part of the diencephalon.
Diencephalic nucleus which is located in the caudal tuberculum. The preglomerular nuclei make up most of the migrated nuclei within the caudal tuberculum. From Neuroanatomy of the Zebrafish Brain.
The portion of tissue that is the initial segment of one of the cranial nerves leaving the central nervous system.
Nucleus located in the midbrain.

Telencephalic nucleus which is in contact with the median ependymal lining of the ventricle and consists of the dorsal, ventral, supracommissural and postcommissural nuclei of the ventral telencephalon. From Neuroanatomy of the Zebrafish Brain.
Telencephalic nucleus which has migrated away from the ependyma and consists of the central, lateral nuclei and the dorsal and ventral entopeduncular nuclei. From Neuroanatomy of the Zebrafish Brain.
Peripheral nucleus of the ventral telencephalon which is located immediately lateral to the dorsal nucleus of the ventral telencephalon. From Neuroanatomy of the Zebrafish Brain.
Diencephalic nucleus which is located between the dorsal hypothalamus and the caudal tuberal nucleus and does not contain tyrosine hydroxylase immunoreactive cells.
Portion of tissue that is the part of the notochord located in the caudal fin.
Epithelium that is the primary epithelium of the developing inner ear and has anterior and posterior maculae as its parts. These maculae later move apart into distinct regions of epithelium.
Portion of tissue that grows in from the dorsolateral wall of the otic vesicle, forming a dorsolateral partition between the developing anterior and posterior semicircular canals.
Podocyte that is part of the pronephros.
Podocyte that is part of the kidney (mesonephros).
Portion of connective tissue that is a fibrous band connecting bones or cartilages or supporting an organ.
Ligament that is in the eye and is located at the angle between the cornea and the iris.
Floor plate that is part of the midbrain.
Multi-tissue structure that consists of the structures that develop into the retina and lens.

comments: Dbxref for ZFIN changed to match actual database accession number.  Old Dbxref ZDB-ANAT-090226-1 correct Dbxref ZDB-ANAT-090227-5.

definition.val: Portion of tissue that gives rise to the lens. The lens of the zebrafish forms by delamination of lens placodal cells and not through invagination. This results in a solid spherical mass as opposed to a hollow lens vesicle. Detachment of the solid lens vesicle of zebrafish at 24-26 hpf is accomplished in part by apoptosis, similar to mammals.

comments: Dbxref for ZFIN changed to match actual database accession number.  Old Dbxref ZDB-ANAT-090226-2 correct Dbxref ZDB-ANAT-090227-6.

definition.val: Diencephalic nucleus that is part of the preoptic area. From Neuroanatomy of the Zebrafish Brain.
Multi-tissue comprised of neurons, dendrites, axon terminals, glial cells, and capillaries.
Multi-tissue structure comprised largely of myelinated axons.
Portion of tissue comprised of four to six layers of nonkeratinized, stratified squamous cells and represents approximately 60% of the thickness of the cornea.
Acellular anatomical structure that is the zone of condensed collagen fibers underneath the basement membrane of the corneal epithelium that overlays the corneal stroma layer.
Portion of tissue that is located basal to Bowman's layer and distal to Descemet's membrane and is comprised largely of connective tissue.

Acellular anatomical structure composed of collagen, Descemet's membrane is secreted as a basement membrane of the corneal endothelium.  Located between the stroma and the corneal endothelium, it is approximately 0.15 micrometer thick at 6 months post-fertilization.
Epithelium that is the innermost layer of the cornea and is comprised of a monolayer of polygonal cells.
Portion of tissue that is part of the eye and gives rise to the mature, fully layered cornea.
Non-material anatomical entity of one dimension, which forms a boundary of an anatomical surface or is a modulation of an anatomical surface.
Anatomical structure that delineates a furrow or depression in an organ or tissue.
Granule cell that is part of the cerebellum.
CNS white matter that crosses the midline within the hindbrain.
Replacement bone that forms within or surrounding a cartilaginous skeletal element.
Granule cell that is part of the olfactory bulb.

nodes.lbl: dorsolateral motor nucleus of vagal nerve

definition.val: Motor nucleus of vagal nerve that is located within the dorsolateral portion of rhombomere 8.

nodes.lbl: medial motor nucleus of vagal nerve

definition.val: Motor nucleus of vagal nerve that is located within the medial portion of rhombomere 8.

Hindbrain nucleus which consists of cell bodies whose axons exit the hindbrain via cranial nerve VIII and innervate hair cells of the lateral line and inner ear. The neurons of these nuclei are unusual in that they extend both contra- and ipsilateral dendrites.
Octaval nerve motor nucleus that is initially located within rhombomere 6.

synonyms.val: rostral cranial nerve VIII motor nucleus

definition.val: Octaval nerve motor nucleus that is initially located within rhombomere 7.

synonyms.val: rostral cranial nerve VIII motor nucleus

definition.val: Multi-tissue structure that is the external layer of the swim bladder that is covered by the peritoneum, and made of elastic fibres and ichthyocol (short needles of collagen).

Multi-tissue structure that is the internal layer of the swim bladder and is comprised of thin endoderm and a smooth muscle layer called the muscularis mucosa with longitudinal and circular fibres with some collagen.
Proliferative region that is part of the developing fin fold or fin.
Portion of tissue that is a thickening and reshaping of the epidermal basal stratum cells beneath which fibroblasts accumulate to form the scale primordium.

A portion of tissue that gives rise to the scale.  At the  initial stages the primordium  is composed of an accumulation of fibroblast below the basil epidermis.   As the primordium develops the epidermis over the fibroblast alters: the basal epidermal cells become cuboidal and narrow, the collagen matrix remodels and the hemidesmosomes disappear.
Duct that is part of the inner ear and connects to the semicircular canals ending in the endolymphatic sac.
Portion of tissue that is part of the corpus cerebelli and contains Purkinje cells.
Multi-tissue structure that has as its parts the medulla oblongata of the hindbrain and the tegmentum of the midbrain.
CNS white matter that is part of the cerebellum.
Portion of tissue that is part of the valvula cerebelli and contains Purkinje cells.

Portion of tissue that consists of afferent axons from the spinal cord, sensory medullary nuclei, reticular nuclei and dorsal tegmental nuclei into the corpus cerebelli and valvula cerebelli, where they synapse within the granule cell layer.
Portion of tissue that consists of afferent axons from the inferior olive and synapsing with Purkinje cells within the molecular layer of the valvula cerebelli and corpus cerebelli.
Portion of tissue that is part of the heart tube and will become the bulbus arteriosus.
Portion of tissue that consists of axons of cerebellar granule cells that synapse onto Purkinje cell dendrites.
Portion of presumptive mesoderm fated to become ventral mesoderm. Determined by fate mapping.
Portion of tissue within the otic vesicle that consists of a thin layer of otic epithelium covering a finger-shaped core of acellular matrix.
Otic vesicle protrusion that is initially ventrally located and then fuses with the lateral protrusion, giving rise to the pillar of the lateral semicircular canal.
Portion of tissue around which the semicircular canals flow.
Multi-tissue structure that is part of the heart tube and will become the cardiac atrium.
Multi-tissue structure that is part of the heart tube and will become the cardiac ventricle.

nodes.lbl: presumptive cardiac ventricle primitive heart tube

definition.val: Multi-tissue structure that is part of the primitive heart tube and will become the cardiac ventricle.
Multi-tissue structure that is part of the primitive heart tube and will become the cardiac atrium.
Portion of tissue that is part of the heart tube and will become the sinus venosus.
Portion of tissue that is part of the heart tube and will become the atrioventricular canal.
Portion of tissue that is part of the heart tube and will become the endocardium.

A glial cell that develops from a Schwann cell precursor. The immature Schwann cell is embedded among neurons (axons) with minimal extracellular spaces separating them from nerve cell membranes and has a basal lamina. Cells can survive without an axon present. Immature Schwann cell can be found communally ensheathing large groups of axons.

comments: Range covers Schwann cell precursor and immature Schwann cell, should be split and put into CL.

definition.val: Artery that extends horizontally across the brain midline which is fed by the caudal division of the internal carotid artery. Three pairs of central arteries extend from the basal communicating artery (BCA), the anterior, middle and  posterior mesencephalic central arteries. The BCA does not correspond to the anterior communicating artery of the mammalian circle of Willis.  The BCA is unpaired.
An artery that connects caudal division of the internal carotid artery and the basal communicating artery to the basilar artery.

synonyms.val: posterior communicating segment of the basilar artery

definition.val: Artery that runs along the ventral keel of the hindbrain.  It lies between the primordial hindbrain channels(PHBC).  It irrigates the hindbrain and is connected, through the hindbrain, to the PHBC by the central arteries.
Is an interconnected tubular multi-tissue structure that contains fluid that is actively transported around the pharyngeal region.
Artery that is part of the pharyngeal arches.
Aortic arch that is part of pharyngeal arch 1. The paired aortic arch 1 arteries branch off of the ventral aorta, and empty into the right and left lateral dorsal aorta.
Aortic arch that is part of pharyngeal arch 2. Aortic arch 2 is paired and is a vestigial structure.

Aortic arch that is part of pharyngeal arch 3. The paired aortic arch 3 arteries branch directly from the ventral aorta to the lateral dorsal aorta, and are retained as the branchial arteries providing circulation through the gills in the adult.

Aortic arch that is part of pharyngeal arch 4. The paired aortic arch 4 arteries branch directly from the ventral aorta to the lateral dorsal aorta, and are retained as the branchial arteries providing circulation through the gills in the adult.

Aortic arch that is part of pharyngeal arch 5. Aortic arch 5 artery is paired and has a common trunk with aortic arch 6 from the ventral aorta, and drains via a separate branch of the lateral dorsal aorta. Aortic arches 5 and 6 supply the trunk and tail with oxygenated blood.

The mesencephalic veins (MsV) enter the mid cerebral veins (MCeV) at the dorsal midline. THe MCeVs originate at the dorsal midline, proceed laterally in a ventral direction to drain into the paired primordial midbrain channels (PHBC).
A vascular loop attached to the efferent part of the mandibular arch that forms at 3.5 dpf.
Artery that enters the gill filament.
The branch of the afferent branchial artery that continues posterodorsally.
The anteroventral proceeding branch of the afferent branchial artery.
Arterioles which take blood from the afferent filamental arteries to the gill lamella.

Aortic arch that is part of pharyngeal arch 6. Aortic arch 6 artery is paired and has a common trunk with aortic arch 5 from the ventral aorta, and drains via a separate branch of the lateral dorsal aorta. Aortic arches 5 and 6 supply the trunk and tail with oxygenated blood.
The vessel between the anterior cerebral vein and the mid cerebral vein.
Artery that exits the gill filament.
Arterioles which take blood from the gill lamella to the efferent  filamental arteries.
Artery that irrigates the forebrain and midbrain.
Extend upward from the PCS, branch to provide an arterial feed to the hindbrain, then drain back down into the PHBC.

The opercular artery arises from the hyoid stump on the first aortic arch, then follows the lateral margin of the operculum (gill covering) ventrally to reconnect into the proximal part of the first aortic arch.
System responsible for generating hematopoietic cells.
Is an interconnected tubular multi-tissue structure that contains fluid that is actively transported around trunk.

Paired trunk vessels that run medially along the dorsal-most portion of the trunk and post-vent region. The DLAVs are formed by the longitudinal anastomoses of the intersegmental vessels to form a right and left pair of dorsal longitudinal anastomotic vessels. These paired vessels remodel to form a single plexiform vessel beginning at 3dpf continuing until after 7dpf.  The basilar artery connects to the DLAVs at the caudal end of the medulla oblongata.  In the early embryo (2.5-3.5 dpf) the primitive hindbrain channels also connect to the DLAV.

comments: Eventually half of the ISVs will carry blood from the aorta to the DLAVs, and half from the DLAV to the posterior cardinal vein.

definition.val: Main route for venous drainage of the anterior and mid cerebral veins as well as portions of the primordial hindbrain channel.
Vein that drains the dorsal longitudinal vessel and the caudal portions of the venous capillary network of the medulla oblongata.
Region where blood progenitor markers are expressed.   Probable site of definitive hematopoiesis between 36hpf and 4dpf.

synonyms.val: dorsal aorta - posterior cardinal vein joint

definition.val: Hematopoietic tissue derived from the cephalic mesoderm.  Predominantly produces myeloid cells.

Vein that branches off the nasal ciliary artery.  Initially, at 2dpf, the dorsal ciliary vein (DCV) drains into the primordial midbrain channel (PMBC) or the PMBC/PHBC junction.  Later, by 3.5dpf,  the DCV drains into the optic choroid vascular plexus.  The dorsal branch of the inner optic circle drains into the DCV.
Vessel that connects to the primitive hindbrain channel and the basilar artery at the caudal end of the medulla oblongata.
Vein that connects to the hyaloid vein that drains the eye.  External to eye.
Is an pair of arteries that form from the intersegmental vessels at the level of the base of the spinal cord, adjacent to the myotome.
Vessels run longitudinally along the horizontal myoseptum.  These vessels are not functional until after lumenization which occurs near 4 dpf.

Vein that drains the vascular plexus of the midgut and hindgut.  At early stages the subintestinal vein (SIV) drains into the PCV and the CCV.  At later stages the SIV drains into the hepatic sinusoid.  The SIV are begin as paired veins.   After 4dpf the remains of the left SIV forms separate segments that empty into the right SIV and eventually merge with the right.  At 3dpf the rostral end of  left SIV begins to form the reticular hepatic sinusoids.  The portion of the left SIV caudal to the hepatic sinusoid forms the hepatic portal vein.
A continuation of the anterior mesenteric artery, which branches out from the dorsal aorta  just caudal to the pronephric glomus.

Is an interconnected tubular multi-tissue structure that contains fluid that is actively transported around the post-vent region. This is frequently referred to as tail vasculature in the zebrafish community.
An ectopic melanophore accumulation analogous to a nevi (mole) in humans.
The artery that carries blood to the eye.  It branches from the primitive internal carotid artery and enters the eye ventrally through the optic fissure.
Artery that is internal to the eye.
Vessels that are associated with the posterior region of the lens.
Vein internal to the eye.
Vessel that branches from cranial division of the internal carotid artery, extending laterally around the rostral end of the head.

After the primitive prosencephalic artery forms it becomes disconnected from the  cranial division of the internal carotid artery and instead it connects to the anterior mesencephalic central artery and the communicating vessels of the palatocerebral artery.
The PLA branches ventrally from the CrDI.  It extends rostrally along the base of mid- and forebrain, then loops medially and links to the corresponding vessel from the other side of the embryo.
Connect the palatocerebral artery with the prosencephalic artery.
Arteries that irrigate rostral regions of the midbrain. They extend between the basal communicating artery to the anterior cerebral vein.
Artery that branches off the cranial division of the internal carotid artery.  It bifurcates near the eye.
Vessel that circles the inner rim of the optic capsule.  The inner optic circle drains ventrally into the optic vein and dorsally into the dorsal ciliary vein.
Drains the PLA loop proceeds straight caudally along the cranial midline just above the pharynx, just below the diencephalon.  Forms a 'T' with the palatocerebral veins.
Vein that drains the median palatocerebral vein into the optic vein.
External layer of the heart.

comments: No evidence of epicardium layer in cardiac atrium in Hu et al 2001.

definition.val: The external layer of the ventricle bounding the ventricular myocardium.  It is formed by a single layer of mesothelial cells supported by a basal lamina, and imbricated with collagen, fibroblasts, and vascular structures in the subepicardial space.

The layer of the myocardium composed of projections of contractile myocytes.   The trabecular layer is bounded internally by the endocardium.  Unlike the trabeculations of higher vertebrates, both atrial and ventricular trabeculae of the zebrafish have more strut-like character, and are more uniform without apparent regional differences.

comments: Cardiac conduction may occur in the trabecular layer via trabecular fibers, PMID: 12626327.

definition.val: Muscular layer of the cardiac ventricle composed of a compact myocardial layer surrounding the trabecular layer.
The outer layer the ventricular myocardium, muscle  fibers are arranged in a compact form and involved the entire ventricle.
Anatomical system located in the heart that initiates and propagates heart contraction.  The cardiac pacemaker.
The atrioventricular valve has four distinct leaflets oriented anterior, posterior, left, and right of the atrioventricular orifice.
Multi-tissue structure composed of leaflets that separate parts of the heart, preventing blood backflow.
The portion of tissue that is the outer layer of the bulbus arteriosus. It is composed of scattered layers of interrupted elastic lamina.
Composed of 7-10 layers of helically arranged smooth muscle cells surrounded by a fine network of collagen, reticular, and elastic fibrils.
Composed of subendothelium with longitudinally oriented collagen, reticular fibrils, elastic fibers, and smooth muscle cells bound by a thin endothelial layer.
Portion of tissue containing  3 and 30 cells  in dorsal right quadrant of atrium that initiate heart beat.
The portion of tissue in the atrioventricular canal that slows impulse propagation between the atrium and cardiac ventricle.

comments: Appears to be mammalian due to exact synonym His-Perkinje system, which doesn't have an exact analog in fish (or frogs). PMID:22984480 PLoS One. 2012;7(9):e44231.

definition.val: A portion of endocardial tissue that is part of the atrioventricular ring.
A ring of conduction tissue that forms in the atrioventricular canal.
Cardiac conduction system, characterized by slow impulse propagation and prolonged refractory periods.
Portion of the cardiac conduction system, with rapid impulse propagation.  In the zebrafish fast conduction takes place in the trabecular layer.
Is a portion of tissue that will develop into vasculature.

The primordial vasculature that will develop into blood vessels  by the process of tubulogenesis.  The  vascular cord is composed of angioblast or vascular endothelial cells in a solid linear mass called a cord.  The cord then undergoes  tubulogenesis to form the lumen of the vessels.
Projects rostrally and dorsally from the AMCtA to irrigate the midportion of the midbrain.
Project dorsally from BCA to irrigate the caudal part of midbrain.
An artery deep in the trunk that connects the dorsal aorta to the supraintestinal artery.

Artery that originates as rostral extensions of the dorsal aorta and travels through the hypophyseal fossa into the cranium.  The internal carotid artery splits near the hypothalamus and optic nerve to form  caudal and cranial divisions.

comments: Homologous to the human ICA ZDB-PUB-140605-9.

definition.val: Branch of the nasal ciliary artery (NCA) that splits off of the NCA near the lens and forms the dorsal ciliary vein.

These arteries branch dorsally from both sides of the posterior communicating artery looping up to join with the opposite MtA as well as with both middle cerebral veins at the dorsal midline of the head.

The nasal arteries start at the internal carotid artery and travel rostrally, passing along the right and left walls of the nasal sac at the most rostral end of the head. From the nasal sac, the NA flows into the nasal veins.

The pectoral (subclavian) arteries originate from the dorsal aorta and takes the blood outward into the fin from the paired portion of the dorsal aorta posterior to the last aortic arch Kimmel et al. 1993.
Vein from the pectoral fin that drains into the ACV just cranial to the ACV-CCV junction.
Branch ventrally from the dorsal aorta and anastomose longitudinally along the dorsal wall of the hindgut, forming the caudal part of the supraintestinal artery.
An elaborate set of vessels that branch off caudally from the anterior mesenteric artery just after the dorsal aorta.  The SBA vascular loops are located on the ventral side of the swim bladder.
Vein that collects blood from the small intestine, pancreas and spleen and routes it through the liver.

Wide thin-walled blood vessels in the liver. The terminal branches of the hepatic portal vein and hepatic artery empty into the sinusoids where the blood mixes. In mammals they have neither venous or arterial markers.  Sinusoids are scattered throughout the parenchyma and appear small and round.

Paired veins that sprout from the dorsal midline junction and dive ventrally.  The mesencephalic veins are fed by the mesencephalic arteries,  after 2.5 dpf, and drain into the dorsal longitudinal vessel after 5dpf.
Vein that  is fed by the nasal artery.  It  rises dorsally around the front of the head, draining into the ACeV and eventually into the PMBC on either side.

Is an ocular vasculature that develops adjacent to the pigment epithelium of the eye. The optic choroid vascular plexus develops from the primitive mesencephalic artery. Initially blood enters from the mesencephalic central artery, and the dorsal ciliary vein. The plexus drains via the ophthalmic vein. The plexus supplies blood to the retina.
Is an interconnected tubular multi-tissue structure that contains fluid that is actively transported around the fin.
Vasculature of the pectoral fin.
Is an interconnected tubular multi-tissue structure that contains fluid that is actively transported around the caudal fin.
Artery that is positioned in the intraray mesenchyme between the hemirays of the lepidotrichia.
Vein that flanks and is just lateral to the fin ray, in the interray mesenchyme.
Vessels that travel between artery and veins or vein and vein in a single ray.
Vessels that travel between veins in adjacent rays.
Stripe of tissue fated to become the median fin fold.
Stripe of tissue on the ventral side of the fish,  fated to become the ventral region of the median fin fold.
Stripe of tissue on the dorsal side of the fish, fated to become the dorsal region of the median fin fold.

A network of blunt ended vessels lacking direct connection to the blood vascular system.  These vessels collect and drain fluids and macromolecules from interstitial spaces throughout the animal.  They derive from a subpopulation of endothelial cells and have walls that are much thinner than the blood carrying vessels.  The walls are mostly composed of single layer of irregularly shaped endothelial cells.  Lymphatic vessels are usually classified as either superficial or deep.

comments: Probably a deep lymph vessel.  This vessel was shown in ZDB-PUB-060616-13 at 5 weeks post fertilization  using Berlin blue dye lymphangiography.

definition.val: Lymph vessel that connects the lateral facial lymph vessel to the thoracic duct near the common cardinal vein.
The thoracic duct is the first perfused lymphatic, located between the dorsal aorta and posterior cardinal vein.

Tract that originates in the telencephalon lies medial to the lateral forebrain bundle and extends into the diencephalon approaching the posterior tuberal nucleus.  In embryos the tract connects cells in the ventral olfactory bulb to those in the septal area.

One of eight distinct lateral lines in the 4-day larva. The dorsal lateral line runs along the dorsal side of the body and never extends beyond the dorsal fin. The lateral line system is stimulated by local water displacements and vibrations, and detects propulsion of the fish through the water, as well as facilitating shoaling, prey capture, and predator and obstacle avoidance. (See Anatomical Atlas entry for lateral line by T. Whitfield.)
Neuromast that is part of the dorsal lateral line. (Also see Anatomical Atlas entry for lateral line by T. Whitfield.)

The middle lateral line system develops from cranial ectodermal placodes, that give rise to both the neuromasts and the middle lateral line sensory nerves that innervate the neuromasts. The middle lateral line system consists of small sensory patches (neuromasts) located superficially on the skin or just under the skin in fluid-filled canals on the body  that are innervated by several lateral line nerves, which project to the hindbrain. The middle  lateral line system is stimulated by local water displacements and vibrations, and detects propulsion of the fish through the water, as well as facilitating shoaling, prey capture, and predator and obstacle avoidance.
Primordium that forms the first neuromasts of the posterior lateral line.  This primordium travels at 1.5-1.7 somites/hour and deposits  neuromast that are polarized along the antero-posterior axis.
Second lateral line primordium.  These primordia move at 0.2 somites/hour and deposit neuromasts that are polarized in a dorso-ventral direction.
A migrating group of cells originating from an anterior lateral line placode.  The primordium deposits neuromasts and interneuromasts between them during its migration.
A migrating group of cells originating from the middle lateral line placode.  The primordium deposits neuromasts  with interneuromasts between them.

A surface structure that will eventually grow to form a barbel.   The primordium is rich in taste buds. The final location for taste buds present in the primordium is the distal tip of the mature barbel.
The serous membrane that lines the peritoneal cavity. The peritoneum secretes serous fluid which allows free movement of the peritoneum and the intraperitoneal organs.
Dorsolateral placode that gives rise to the middle lateral line.
Dorsal region of the axial hypoblast.
Epithelium lining the lumen of the gut.
Simple columnar epithelium that lines the intestine, sometimes pseudostratified, with absorptive brush-border cells and mucous goblet cells clearly visible.
The entire digestive tract is lined by well developed villi, which are longest in the intestinal bulb and decrease progressively in size towards the caudal end of the intestine.
Intestinal epithelium which lines the lumen of the intestinal bulb.
Intestinal epithelium which lines the lumen of the mid intestine.
Intestinal epithelium which lines the lumen of the posterior intestine.
A double layer composed of peritoneum that attaches the visceral organs to the body wall.
The mesentery that originates from the dorsal side of the peritoneal cavity.
The peritoneum that lines the walls of the peritoneal cavity.
The part of peritoneum that is a serous membrane covers the external surfaces of most abdominal organs.
The mesentery that originates from the ventral side of the peritoneal cavity.
Epithelium that is part of a developing tooth or dental organ.
Epithelium that surrounds a primary tooth.  This epithelium is committed to formation of replacement teeth.
Inner layer of the dental organ, composed of ameloblasts.
Outer layer of the dental organ.

A structure that is involved in tooth formation.  A bell shape two layer  epithelial structure that descends toward the ceratobranchial 5 bone, enclosing the dental mesenchyme.  At later times in tooth development the cells at the apex of the bell may be involved in enameloid formation.  This structure remains at the base of the erupted tooth and is the source of replacement teeth.
Mesenchyme enclosed by dental organ.
Tissue of the developing tooth. It is composed of pre-odontoblast and undifferentiated mesenchyme cells and connects to the basal lamina.
Material in the center of the dentine of the mature tooth.  In secondary teeth the pulp contains blood vessels and odontoblast.
A dentine-like hypermineralized substance that covers the zebrafish tooth tip.
Mineralized collagen containing substance that forms the tooth structure.  The dentine of first-generation teeth of zebrafish is entirely atubular.
Nerve that is a branch of cranial nerve VIII and innervates the cristae ampullaris.
Portion of tissue composed of contractile fibers.

An area of medulla oblongata, outside the blood-brain barrier.  The apical terminals of the neurons are, in a strategic location either to monitor the chemical constitution of the plasma or to release neurogenic substances into the general circulation.
A portion of tissue that is regrowing after damage or amputation.
Part of the regeneration epithelium closest to the fin.
Part of regeneration epithelium at the tip of the fin.
Layer of the wound epidermis that surrounds the blastema.
Iridescent iridophore containing gold reflecting platelets.
Iridescent iridophore containing silver reflecting platelets.
Is a joint that is located at the point of contact of articulating bones allowing movement. The joint has a capsule containing synovial fluid surrounding the articulating bone surfaces.
Portion of organism substance that is a clear fluid that occupies the space in synovial joints.
Is a joint where the ends of two bones are connected by cartilage.
A joint where fibrous tissue such as ligament connects two calcified tissues.
Tela chorioidea that lines the tectal ventricle.
Tela chorioidea that lines the fourth ventricle.
Tela chorioidea that lines the third ventricle.
Tela chorioidea that lines the telencephalic ventricle.
Artery that supplies blood to the liver.
Ducts that transport enzymes between organs in the digestive system.
Intercellular space surrounded by the basal surface of the hepatocytes that collect bile.

comments: Should be located in rather then part_of when we get the appropriate relation.

definition.val: Digestive system duct that collects bile from the canaliculus and transports bile through the liver.
Digestive system duct that collects bile from the hepatic bile duct and the cystic duct.
Duct that connects the gall bladder and the common bile duct.
Ampulla formed by the union of the pancreatic duct and the common bile duct where they enter the intestine.
Vein that is central to a lobule in the liver.
Duct that collects bile from the bile ductules and connects to the extrahepatic bile duct.
Bile duct that collects from the intrahepatic ducts and feeds into the common bile duct.
A tube shaped multi-tissue structure lined with epithelial cells that collects secretions and routes them to their destination.
Portion of the liver which develops on the fish's left side.
Portion of the liver which develops on the fish's right side adjacent to the gall bladder.
Portion of the liver which develops on the fish's ventral side.

An interneuron in the spinal cord.  CoPA are bipolar with "T" shaped dorsal dendrites, have contralateral longitudinal axons that crosses the cord dorsal to the Mauthner neuron and ascends in the dorsal cord. These neurons are glutamatergic.

comments: This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

definition.val: An interneuron of the spinal cord. CoSA,  have contralateral longitudinal axons that crosses the cord dorsal to the Mauthner neuron and ascends in the dorsal cord.  These neurons are glutamatergic and glycinergic.

comments: This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

definition.val: An interneuron of the spinal cord. VeLD neurons have an ipsilateral longitudinal axon.

comments: This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

definition.val: An interneuron of the spinal cord.  These are ascending and glycinergic.

comments: This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

definition.val: A primary motor neuron that is part of the spinal cord.   The axon turns caudally and dorsally, coursing between the surface of the spinal cord and the medial surface of the axial muscles to the dorsal median septum. The MiP axon then extends dorsally to the surface of the muscle, where it turns to arborize ventrally over the lateral surface of the axial muscle.

comments: This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

definition.val: A primary motoneuron that is part of the spinal cord.  The axon of the CaP motoneuron on emerging from the ventral root.  At the horizontal septum, however, the RoP axon turns laterally to extend within the horizontal septum to the lateral surface of the muscle.

comments: This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

definition.val: A primary motor neuron of the spinal cord. This cell is present in slightly less than half of the trunk hemisegments. Additionally, this cell has at least 2 different fates: it may become a primary motoneuron and arborize in an exclusive muscle territory, or it may die during embryonic development.

comments: This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

definition.val: Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 5 with one dorsal neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.  The axonal projection is ipsilateral.

comments: This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

definition.val: Hindbrain interneuron, part of rhombomere 5, with one dorsal, caudal-lateral neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.  The fibers pass through the lateral longitudinal fasciculus.

comments: This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

definition.val: Hindbrain interneuron, part of rhombomere 5, with one dorsal, caudal-medial neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.  The fibers pass through the medial longitudinal fasciculus.

comments: This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

definition.val: A bifurcate interneuron.  It is a commissural neuron with both ascending and descending axons in the ventrolateral cord contralateral to its cell body.

comments:
This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

This may be the same as CoBL.

definition.val: Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 4, with one medial neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 4 with one rostral neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 5 with one rostral neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 4 with six ventral neurons in each hemi-rhombomere.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 5 with five ventral neurons in each hemi-rhombomere.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 2 with two intermediate-caudal  neurons in each hemi-rhombomere.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 2 with one intermediate-rostral neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.

Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 1 with twelve neurons in each hemi-rhombomere. Axon runs in the dorsal spinal cord. The RoL1 stem axon projects ipsilaterally along the brain stem and spinal cord. From the more lateral location of its spinal axon, RoL1's collaterals projected both ventrally and medially.  The branching of RoL1 collaterals within the medulla, occur at multiple levels.

comments:
May have 12  neurons in each hemi rhombomere ZDB-PUB-060217-9.
7 neurons in each hemi rhombomere 080227-17 and 931014-770.

definition.val: Hindbrain interneuron, part of rhombomere 6, with one caudal-lateral neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.  The fibers pass through the lateral longitudinal fasciculus.

comments: This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

definition.val: Hindbrain interneuron, part of rhombomere 6, with one caudal-medial neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.  The fibers pass through the medial longitudinal fasciculus.

comments: This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

definition.val: Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 6 with one dorsal neuron in each hemi-rhombomere. The axonal projection is ipsilateral.

comments: This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

definition.val: Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 2 with 4 lateral neurons in each hemi-rhombomere.
The rostral RoL2 interneuron that is part of rhombomere 2.
The caudal RoL2 interneuron that is part of rhombomere 2.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 3 with 1 lateral neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 1 with one medial-caudal neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 1 with one medial-rostral neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 3 with three ventral neurons in each hemi-rhombomere.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 2 with one medial-lateral neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 2 with one medial neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 3 with one lateral neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 32 with two medial neurons in each hemi-rhombomere.
Is a CNS interneuron that is part of the hindbrain.
CNS interneurons located in the midbrain.
Midbrain interneuron in the lateral midbrain.
Midbrain interneuron located in the medial midbrain.  Neuron bodies are part of a cluster.
Midbrain interneuron at the rostral position in the lateral midbrain.
Midbrain interneuron at the caudal position in the lateral midbrain.
Midbrain interneuron at the medial position in the lateral midbrain.
Midbrain interneuron located in the medial midbrain.  The neuron body is positioned away from the MeM cluster.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 7, with 2 ventral neurons in each hemi-rhombomere.
Hindbrain interneuron that is part of rhombomere 7, with 1 dorsal neuron in each hemi-rhombomere.
Cranial vasculature where the posterior cerebral veins and the trans-choroid plexus branch form a circuit around the 4th ventricle choroid plexus.
A melanophore stripe that forms the immature pigment pattern.
A larval melanophore stripe that forms a band extending from the head to the tail along the dorsal apex of the myotomes.

A larval melanophore stripe that forms a band, of pigmentation extending from the bases of the eyes on each side of the midline to the top of the tail, that underlies the myotomes bilaterally, still is dorsal to the yolk.
The larval melanophore stripe that is at the level of the horizontal myoseptum.
The larval melanophore stripe that forms a band over the ventral surface of the yolk sac.
Lip that covers the lower portion of the mouth.
Lip that covers the upper portion of the mouth.
A group of about 20 cells deposited by the migrating lateral line primordium. A few hours after deposition these cells mature to form the neuromast.
A portion of tissue that contains the choroid vasculature. It lies at the back of the eye.
A bifurcate interneuron where the axon projects ventrally, crossing the spinal cord  ventral to the Mauthner axons before bifurcating and extending dorsally.
Is a spinal interneuron that has an axon that splits into roughly equal size branches.
A multipolar  interneuron with branching dendrites.  The axon extend ventrally, lateral to the Mauthner axons before crossing the cord and descending ventrally.
A interneuron of the spinal cord with an ipsilateral axon that extends ventral to the Mauthner axon then turns dorsally  and caudally and sometimes has a minor ascending branch.

A spinal interneuron with a dorsal dendrite that extends caudally. CoLA also has  midlateral process that extend rostrally and caudally, the axon crosses the cord dorsal to the Mauthner cell and ascends, extending dorsally.  Axon has minor descending branch.  These neurons are glycinergic.

A unipolar spinal interneuron that forms dendritic trunk laterally with extensive dendritic branching.  The axon extends ventrally, lateral to the Mauthner axons before crossing the cord and descending ventrally.

A nucleated immature erythrocyte, having cytoplasm generally similar to that of the earlier proerythroblast but sometimes even more basophilic, and usually regular in outline. The nucleus is still relatively large, but the chromatin strands are thicker and more deeply staining, giving a coarser appearance; the nucleoli have disappeared.
A nucleated precursor of an erythrocyte.

A smooth muscle cell that synthesizes, stores, and secretes the enzyme renin. This cell type are located in the wall of the afferent arteriole at the entrance to the glomerulus. While having a different origin than other kidney smooth muscle cells, this cell type expresses smooth muscle actin upon maturation.

Is a specialized pore forming cell of the follicle, located adjacent to the animal pole of the oocyte.  The micropylar cell makes the single micropyle (pore) through the chorion through which the sperm fertilizes the egg.

Kolmer-Agduhr neurons are ciliated GABAergic neurons that contact the central canal of the spinal cord and have ipsilateral ascending axons. KA neurons are the dorsal population of CSF-cNs that originate from pMN. The apical extension of dorsal CSF-cNs is more extended along the border of the central canal  than for ventral CSF-cNs.
