Dorsal CSF-cNs contact V0-v cells and project onto CoPA sensory interneuron and respond to lateral bending of the spinal cord.

A nucleated, immature erythrocyte in which the nucleus occupies a relatively smaller part of the cell than in its precursor, the basophilic erythroblast. The cytoplasm is beginning to acquire hemoglobin and thus is no longer a purely blue color, but takes on an acidophilic tint, which becomes progressively more marked as the cell matures. The chromatin of the nucleus is arranged in coarse, deeply staining clumps.
A ciliated epithelial cell with many cilia.
A ciliated epithelial cell with a single cilium.
A non-sensory cell that extends from the basement membrane to the apical surface of the auditory epithelium and provides support for auditory hair cells.
A non-terminally differentiated cell that originates from the neural crest and differentiates into a xanthophore.
Is a fibroblast that differentiates from the de-differentiated cells of the blastema.

Interneurons of the spinal cord that arise from the V1 proliferation domain.  The CiA neurons inhibit motoneurons, other ventral interneurons and also inhibit dorsal sensory neurons involved in sensory gating.  CiA have ipsilateral axonal projections in both motor and sensory regions of spinal cord.
Vein segment that connects the PCeVs in the choroid plexus.
Is an interconnected tubular multi-tissue structure that contains fluid that is actively transported around the organism.
Microvessel of the blood circulation system lined with vascular endothelial cells.
A portion of tissue that consists of a tube composed of epithelial cells that connects larger vessels.
Microvessel of the lymphatic system lined with a single layer of overlapping endothelial cells.
Simple squamous epithelium that lines the capsule of the renal glomerulus.
Portion of tissue that encapsulates the glomerulus of the renal corpuscle.
Multi-tissue structure part of the blood vasculature. Feeds into capillaries.   Arterioles carry blood away from the heart.

A portion of tissue formed between the base of the intestinal villi that is part of the intestinal epithelium where intestinal stem cells are located.  In the intestine no crypts are present, but, the regions between the villi, the intervillus pockets, have a crypt-like function. Cells are produced in the intervillus pockets and  shed from the villus tips.
The vascular endothelium that lines blood vasculature.
The vascular endothelium that lines the lymph vessels.
Blood vessel endothelium that has tight junctions between the endothelial cells.
Blood vessel endothelium that has pores that allow passage small molecules and proteins.
Fenestrated blood vessel endothelium with pores that are large enough for blood cells to pass.  The basal lamina and gap junctions  may be discontinuous.
Caudal procurrent ray that is dorsally located on the caudal fin skeleton.  There are 6 dorsal procurrent rays in the caudal fin.
Caudal procurrent ray that is ventrally located on the caudal fin skeleton.  There are 5 ventral procurrent rays in the caudal fin.
Ligament that connects the maxilla with the lower jaw.
Ligament that connects the premaxilla to the maxilla.

comments: The DLF in zebrafish is not homologous to the Mammalian DLF.  Mammalian DLF is in brain, the fish DLF is in spinal cord.

definition.val: Muscle that moves parts of the body in a superior direction to an erect position.  Antagonistic to depressor muscle.
A muscle that brings a body part closer to the sagittal plane of the body.
A muscle that moves a body part away  from the sagittal plane of the body.
A muscle that moves body parts into an inferior direction or lowered position.  Antagonistic to erector muscle.
A muscle that decrease the angle of a joint.

Immature reproductive tissue that has not undergone final maturation into either testis or ovary. During gonadal development, the gonad may be  transiently intersexual where there is a  parallel presence of both oocytes and spermatocytes.
A thin layer of smooth muscle immediately beneath the base of the villi which permits the intestinal mucosa to dynamically move and fold.
A non-striated muscle that is composed of spindle-shaped cells.  Smooth muscle usually is organized into sheets that line cavitated organs.
Fin muscle that is located on the anterior edge of the fin. Arrector muscles change the angle of the leading edge of the fin during swimming.
Muscle, composed of long cylindrical, multinucleated cells that attaches to the skeleton via tendons.
Smooth muscle of the enteric muscle system in which the cells are aligned parallel to the lumen of the gut.
Smooth muscle of the enteric muscle system in which the cells are aligned perpendicular to the lumen of the gut.
Muscle composed of cardiac muscle cells that is part of the heart.
The part of the nephron that filters blood.
Functional unit of the kidney that filters waste.
Space between the renal glomerulus and the glomerular capsule.  Renal filtrate collects here then passes into the renal tubule.
Capillary that is part of the renal glomerulus. Glomerular capillary endothelial membranes in zebrafish normally have a fenestrated morphology that is visible at the ultrastructural level.

Membrane between the mesonephric podocytes and the renal glomerular capillaries.  It is a trilaminar structure composed of the fused basement membranes of the glomerular capillaries and basement membrane of the podocytes.  The capillary membranes touch the GBM at contact points that are separated by pores.  The membrane is semi-permeable allowing water and small molecules to pass into the renal capsular space.
Middle layer of the glomerular basement membrane.
Layer of the glomerular basement membrane adjacent to the glomerular capillary.
Layer of the glomerular basement membrane adjacent to the podocytes and capsular space.
Tubes in the kidney that collect and transport urine.
Renal tubule that connects to the renal corpuscle.
Portion of the renal tubule which is just posterior to the proximal convoluted tubule.
Portion of the renal tubule which is just posterior to the distal early tubule.
Portion of the renal tubule which is just posterior to the proximal straight tubule.
Renal duct that collects the output from renal tubules.
Blood vessel that runs along the rostral-caudal axis of the fish.
Lymph vessel that runs along the rostral-caudal axis of the fish.
Blood vessel part of cranial vasculature.
Lymph vessel that is located in the head.
Blood vessel part of fin.
Lymph vessel part of fin.
Blood vessel part of pectoral fin.
Lymph vessel part of pectoral fin.
Lymph vessel that is located in the caudal fin.
Blood vessel located within the caudal fin.
Is an interconnected tubular multi-tissue structure that contains fluid that is actively transported around the kidney.
Blood vessel part of kidney.
Arteriole that carries blood into the glomerular capillaries.
Arteriole that carries blood out of the glomerular capillaries.
Capillary that is part of the pronephric glomerulus. Glomerular capillary endothelial membranes in zebrafish normally have a fenestrated morphology that is visible at the ultrastructural level.
Portion of tissue that encapsulates the glomerulus of the pronephros.
Simple squamous epithelium that lines the capsule of the pronephric glomerulus.
Space between the pronephric glomerulus and the pronephric glomerular capsule.  Renal filtrate collects here then passes into the pronephric tubule.

Membrane between the pronephric podocytes and the pronephric glomerular capillaries.  It is a trilaminar structure composed of the fused basement membranes of the glomerular capillaries and basement membrane of the podocytes.  The capillary membranes touch the GBM at contact points that are separated by pores.  The membrane is semi-permeable allowing water and small molecules to pass into the renal capsular space.
Any of the tubular multi-tissue structures through which blood circulates in the body.
Multi-tissue structure part of the blood vasculature formed by the anastomosis of capillaries.  Venules carry blood toward the heart.
Fin fold of the pectoral fin bud, resulting from mesenchyme migration into the apical fold.

The functional webs of the paired fins.  They arise as a consequence of apical fold (AER) expansion and of migration of the distal mesenchyme. The fin folds are delimited by a dorsal and a ventral striated epidermis. The cells of the epidermal basal stratum are positioned on a basement membrane. Directly beneath the basement membrane of each side there is an array of actinotrichs, fibers of the extracellular matrix which act as supportive elements of the larval fin folds.

comments: Does not exist in teleosts.  Menke, A.L., Spitsbergen, J.M., Wolterbeek, A.P., and Woutersen, R.A. (2011) Normal Anatomy and Histology of the Adult Zebrafish. Toxicol. Pathol. 39(5): 759-775.

definition.val: Lymph vessel that is located between the somites.
Smooth muscle that is part of the blood vessel wall.

A cuboidal epithelial cell of the kidney which regulates sodium and potassium balance. The activity of sodium and potassium channels on the cells apical membrane is regulated by aldosterone and vasopressin. In mammals these cells are located in the renal collecting ducts.

Specialized epithelial cells involved in the maintenance of osmotic homeostasis. They are characterized by abundant mitochondria and ion transporters. In amniotes, they are present in the renal system. In freshwater fish, ionocytes in the skin and gills help maintain osmotic homeostasis by absorbing salt from the external environment.
Ionocyte located in the gills.
Ionocyte located in the integument during embryonic and larval stages.
Integument ionocyte rich in Na,K-ATPase.
Integument ionocyte rich in vacuolar-type H+ ATPase and carbonic anhydrase.
A non-terminally differentiated cell that originates from the neural crest and differentiates into an iridophore.
A non-terminally differentiated cell that originates from the neural crest and differentiates into a leucophore.
A non-terminally differentiated cell that originates from the neural crest and differentiates into an erythrophore.
A non-terminally differentiated cell that originates from the neural crest and differentiates into a cyanophore.

A multi-tissue mediosagittal cylinder-shaped structure, clearly isolated from the jaw and adhered by differentiated plicae to the lateral walls.  It is composed of a muscle surrounded by a squamous epithelium.
The thick, high squamous-stratified epithelium that covers the lips.  It contains abundant melanocytes, many mucous cells and has well differentiated taste buds.
Embryonic structure that forms from the anterior gut epithelium at the level of the second somite that then matures to form the swim bladder.
Portion of the gut endoderm that will eventually develop into the swim bladder.
Portion of tissue that forms from the swim bladder epithelium at the anterior end of the swim bladder.
Posterior protrusion of the third ventricle.
Is a diencephalic nucleus located in the caudal periventricular hypothalamus surrounding the lateral recess of the third ventricle.
A diencephalic nucleus that is part of caudal periventricular hypothalmus surrounding the posterior recess of the third ventricle.

CNS white matter tract, major catecholaminergic tract that originates in the diencephalon, passes the locus coeruleus and projects through the hindbrain into the spinal cord.  Dopaminergic neurons of the posterior tuberculum and lateral hypothalamus provide the predominant contribution to descending mlct projections.

CNS white matter, an axonal tract between ventral diencephalon and ventral telencephalon.  At 3 d, the anterior catecholaminergic tract includes noradrenergic projections from the hindbrain and dopaminergic projections from ventral dopaminergic neurons. During later development, other catecholaminergic projections may also contribute to the anterior catecholaminergic tract.
CNS white matter, axonal tract interconnecting catecholaminergic cell groups in the posterior tuberculum and hypothalamus.
CNS white matter, axonal tract between the preoptic region and ventral diencephalon.

Connective tissue in which fat is stored. All adipose tissue in the zebrafish is white adipose tissue and is comprised of adipocytes, stroma, macrophages and vasculature.  Mature adipocytes express adipokines, such as adipsin, adiponectin, and leptin so white adipose tissue is considered an endocrine organ. The connective tissue is loose and infiltrated by a vascular network.
Portion of the pancreatic duct, external to the pancreas body that connects the pancreas with the hepatopancreatic ampulla.
Vertebra that is posteriorly adjacent to vertebra 4.
Vertebra that is posteriorly adjacent to vertebra 5.
Vertebra that is posteriorly adjacent to vertebra 6.
Vertebra that is posteriorly adjacent to vertebra 7.
Vertebra that is posteriorly adjacent to vertebra 8.
Vertebra that is posteriorly adjacent to vertebra 11.  May have hemal arch and rib.
Vertebra that is posteriorly adjacent to vertebra 9.
Vertebra that is posteriorly adjacent to vertebra 10.
First lepidotrichium of dorsal fin.
Second lepidotrichium of dorsal fin.
Third lepidotrichium of dorsal fin.
Fourth lepidotrichium of dorsal fin.
Fifth lepidotrichium of dorsal fin.
Sixth lepidotrichium of dorsal fin.
Seventh lepidotrichium of dorsal fin.
First proximal radial of the dorsal fin.
Second proximal radial of the dorsal fin.
Dorsal fin pterygiophore that is the rostralmost pterygiophore of the dorsal fin.
Dorsal fin pterygiophore that is caudal to  pterygiophore 1 of the dorsal fin.
Dorsal fin pterygiophore that is caudal to pterygiophore 2 of the dorsal fin.
Dorsal fin pterygiophore that is caudal to  pterygiophore 3 of the dorsal fin.  One of the two first radials to ossify.
Dorsal fin pterygiophore that is caudal to  pterygiophore 4 of the dorsal fin. One of the two first radials to ossify.
Dorsal fin pterygiophore that is caudal to  pterygiophore 5 of the dorsal fin.
Dorsal fin pterygiophore that is caudal to  pterygiophore 6 of the dorsal fin.
Dorsal fin pterygiophore that is caudal to  pterygiophore 7 of the dorsal fin. The most caudal pterygiophore of the dorsal fin.
First distal radial of the dorsal fin.
Second distal radial of the dorsal fin.
Third distal radial of the dorsal fin.
Fourth distal radial of the dorsal fin.
Fifth distal radial of the dorsal fin.
Sixth distal radial of the dorsal fin.
Seventh distal radial of the dorsal fin.
Eighth distal radial of the dorsal fin.
Third proximal radial of the dorsal fin.
Fourth proximal radial of the dorsal fin.
Fifth proximal radial of the dorsal fin.
Sixth proximal radial of the dorsal fin.
Seventh proximal radial of the dorsal fin.
Eighth proximal radial of the dorsal fin.
Anatomical space that is an opening in a bone, usually for passage of blood vessels or nerves.
Foramen in the occipital region of neurocranium to allow passage of spinal cord.
Anatomical space that is a concavity, shallow depression, or groove on a bone.

Anatomical space that is the external opening of a gill slit connecting the pharyngeal cavity to the exterior. Gill opening is usually paired and located on the lateral or ventral surfaces of the head.
Gill ray is a slender, usually rod-like cartilage within a gill filament and ligamentously tied to its associated pharyngeal arch bone or cartilage.
Lateral line system on head and pectoral girdle.

Sensory canal associated with the infraorbital bones. Located in close proximity to the otic and supraorbital sensory canals at their union. The infraorbital sensory canal is bilaterally paired.  The canal has 3 pores.  The first opens dorsally below the nasal sac, the second opens laterally posterior to the anterior maxilary barbel, the third pore is present where infraorbital 1 meets infraorbital 2.

Sensory canal between and in close proximity posteriorly with the post-otic and preoperculo-mandibular sensory canals, and anteriorly with the supraorbital and infraorbital sensory canals. The otic sensory canal is bilaterally paired.

Sensory canal  that forms dorsal to the horizontal semicircular canal of the ear. The anterior end of the canal may span or remain posterior to the pterotic-sphenotic junction. The posterior end of the postotic canal meets the temporal and supratemporal canals. The canal lies between and in close proximity to the trunk sensory canal and anteriorly with the otic and preoperculo-mandibular sensory canals. The post-otic sensory canal is bilaterally paired and when mature is fully enclosed in bone.

Sensory canal lateral or ventral to and in close proximity to the otic and post-otic sensory canals at their union. The preoperculo-mandibular sensory canal is bilaterally paired and associated with the preopercle, angular and dentary bones.

Sensory canal contained in the nasal and frontal bones.  It contains 4 or 5 neuromast. The first neuromast is positioned in the tubular nasal bone and the other neuromasts are located in the frontal bone.  The canal is anterior to and in close proximity to the otic and infraorbital sensory canals. The supraorbital sensory canal is bilaterally paired.
Nerve that is a branch of cranial nerve VII that passes through or around the suspensorium to innervate muscles and sensory organs on or near pharyngeal arch 2.
Actinotrichium that is part of the anal fin skeleton.
First lepidotrichium of anal fin.
Second lepidotrichium of anal fin.
Third epidotrichium of anal fin.
Fourth lepidotrichium of anal fin.
Fifth lepidotrichium of anal fin.
Sixth lepidotrichium of anal fin.
Seventh lepidotrichium of anal fin.
Fossa or chamber on anterior portion of orbital region of neurocranium that serves as attachment site for anterior extrinsic eye musculature.

comments: Dbxref for ZFIN changed to match actual database accession number.  Old Dbxref ZDB-ANAT-100614-560 correct Dbxref ZDB-ANAT-100614-60.

definition.val: Barbel that is associated with the anterior nostril. Anterior nasal barbel is paired.

Barbel that is attached to the distal end of the maxilla and that originates on the side of the mouth. Maxillary barbel is paired. Maxillary barbel has considerable range of motion, with contraction and protraction motivated by maxillary blood sinus. The maxillary barbel integument has taste buds. Capable of regeneration.
The dorsal part of the ear. Comprises the three semicircular canals, which connect to the utricle.
The ventral portion of the ear.  It contains the saccule and the lagena which are the primary auditory organs.
Duct that forms when the nonampullary ends of the posterior and anterior semicircular canals merge.  The common crus descends connecting the semicircular canals to the horizontal canal.
Duct that crosses the midline connecting the sacculi.
Portion of organism substance that is an extracellular fluid that has a low potassium concentration.

The portion of organism substance that is an extracellular fluid that has a high potassium and sodium concentration. It bathes the hair cells and is thought to be generated by the semicircular canal epithelium.
Foramen in the inner ear.
Inner ear foramen that connects the lagena to the caudal half of the saccule.
Inner ear foramen that connects the rostral saccula to the utricle.
Opening in the neurocranium to allow communication between the swim bladder and the inner ear.
Foramen through which cranial nerve VIII (auditory) exits.
Joint that articulates the autopalatine and lateral ethmoid and bones. Autopalatine-lateral ethmoid joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the autopalatine cartilage or bone and maxilla.
Joint that articulates autopalatine and vomer bones. Autopalatine vomer joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the basioccipital and exoccipital. Basioccipital exoccipital joint is paired.
Actinotrichium that is part of the caudal fin skeleton.
A multi-tissue structure that is comprised of a secretory epithelial layer and a connective tissue layer.
Foramen that is located in the coracoid.
Foramen located on the dentary for passage of the mandibular nerve.
Foramen in the orbital region of the neurocranium for the passage of the optic nerve (cranial nerve II).
Foramen for the passage of the olfactory nerve or cranial nerve 1.
Foramen in the bone or cartilage comprising the anterior wall of the orbit, and through which passes at least the deep ophthalmic branch of cranial nerve V (trigeminal).
Foramen that is located in the scapula. The scapular foramen is paired and formed by a ring of bone.
Foramen through which the superficial ophthalmic nerve passes.
Barbel that originates on the chin.
Acellular anatomical structure that is a natural outgrowth, prominence, or point that is part of the surface of a tooth.

Skeletal element which is a slender, horny, flexible, unsegmented fibril which provide structural support to fin folds or fins. Actinotrichia are translucent, exhibit birefringence (double refraction) and are composed of the scleroprotein elastoidine.
Nerve that is part of the pectoral fin.
Motor nerve that is located in the pectoral fin.
Sensory nerve that innervates the pectoral fin.
Pectoral fin motor nerve that enters the fin from the dorsal side of the fin rostral of all other motor nerves and innervates the dorsal side of the pectoral fin.
Pectoral fin motor nerve that enters the fin from the dorsal side of the fin caudal of nerve 1 and rostral of all other motor nerves and innervates the dorsal side of the pectoral fin.
Pectoral fin motor nerve that enters the fin from the dorsal side of the fin caudal of nerve 1 and 2 and rostral of nerve 4 and innervates the dorsal side of the pectoral fin.
Fossa of opercle that articulates with hyomandibular condyle for opercle.
Multi-tissue structure that is a fluid-filled canal in the integument and usually dermal bones, and contains one or more neuromasts.
Sensory canal on trunk which is posterior to the pectoral girdle and often reaching to the caudal fin. The trunk sensory canal is bilaterally paired.
Sensory canal that crosses over the dorsal midline and in close proximity to the contralateral post-otic sensory canals. The supratemporal sensory canal is unpaired.
Foramen in a bone that is part of a sensory canal.
Sensory canal that is a side branch between a sensory canal proximally and a pore at its distal end. Sensory canal tubule is bilaterally paired although often imperfectly so.

Sensory canal associated with the preopercle bone. It lies in close proximity with the otic and post-otic sensory canals at their union, and anteroventrally with the mandibular sensory canal. The preopercular sensory canal is bilaterally paired.
An anatomical space that is an opening, usually at the end of a canal or duct, on the surface of the integument or the lining of an internal anatomical space.
Pectoral fin motor nerve that enters the fin from the ventral side of the fin and innervates the ventral side of the pectoral fin.

Sensory canal associated with the angular and dentary bones.  Located anterior and ventral to and in close proximity with the preoperculo-mandibular sensory canal. The mandible sensory canal is bilaterally paired and does not meet at the mandibular symphysis. The mandibular lateral line canal has 3 neuromast located between the canal pores.  Two neuromast are located in the portion of the can in the dentary bone.  The third neuromast is located in the anguloarticular bone.  The rostral end of the canal has a terminal pore adjacent to the mandibular symphysis. There are three more pores, two, at the posterior of the dentary and the anterior of the anguloarticular share a common epithelial pore.

Tract which originates from several clusters of cell bodies in the rostral midbrain, the anterior tegmentum and the nucleus of the posterior commissure.  The neurons that are part of the nucleus of the posterior commissure have axons which course ventrally toward the anterior tegmentum, while the ventral tegmental clusters of neurons have axons which course dorsally along the posterior commissure and cross the dorsal midline to the contralateral side they then turn posteriorly and extend to the posterior region of the tegmentum and eventually into the hindbrain via the tract of the postoptic commissure. .
Opening in the prootic.  The basicapsular fenestra is paired.
Fossa where the anteromedial rim is formed by the prootic, the remainder of the fossa is formed by the exoccipital and pterotic.
Foramen formed between the anterolateral extension of the prootic that contacts the pterosphenoid the prootic and the pterosphenoid.
Perilymphatic compartment of the zebrafish ear. Chamber positioned ventral of the medulla, the anterior end surrounds the posterior portion of the transverse canal.
Joint that articulates the dorsal hypohyal and urohyal bones. Dorsal hypohyal-urohyal joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the dorsal hypohyal and ventral hypohyal bones. Dorsal hypohyal-ventral hypohyal joint is paired.
Space that contains perilymph.
Bilaterally paired perilymphatic chambers posterior to the sinus impar.  These connect to the anterior end of the otic duct.
Duct that contains perilymph.
Pair of perilymphatic ducts that extend from the dorsal ends of the atria of the sinus impar, pass on either side of the medulla and merge and extend dorsally toward the surface of the head.
Ectoderm of the oral region.
Joint that articulates the dentary and anguloarticular. Dentary-anguloarticular joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the ceratohyal and branchiostegal ray bones. Ceratohyal-branchiostegal ray joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the ceratohyal and ventral hypohyal bones. Ceratohyal-ventral hypohyal joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the ceratohyal and dorsal hypohyal bones. Ceratohyal-dorsal hypohyal joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the frontal and pterotic. Frontal-pterotic joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the hyomandibula bone and metapterygoid bone.
Joint between the right and left coracoid bones of the pectoral girdle.
Joint that articulates the left and right basipterygium cartilage or bone. Inter-basipterygium joint is unpaired.
Joint between the left and right frontal bone.
Joint that articulates the interhyal and epihyal bones, or interhyal and ceratohyal cartilages. Interhyal-epihyal joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the left and right hypobranchial 3 cartilages or bones. Inter-hypobranchial 3 joint is unpaired.
Joint that articulates the left and right premaxillary bones. Inter-premaxillary joint is unpaired.
Joint that articulates the contralateral ventral hypohyal bones or the contralateral ceratobranchial cartilages. Inter-ventral hypohyal joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the lateral ethmoid and frontal. Lateral ethmoid-frontal joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the opercle and interopercle. Opercle-interopercle joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the orbitosphenoid cartilage or bone and lateral ethmoid cartilage or bone. Orbitosphenoid-lateral ethmoid joint is paired.
Joint that articulates orbitosphenoid and prootic bones. Orbitosphenoid-prootic joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the prootic and exoccipital. Prootic-exoccipital joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the prootic and pterosphenoid. Prootic-pterosphenoid joint is paired.  The parasphenoid borders the prootic medially in a syndesmosis.
Joint that articulates the pterosphenoid and orbitosphenoid. Pterosphenoid-orbitosphenoid joint is paired.
Joint that articulates quadrate and anguloarticular bones. Quadrate-articular joint is paired.
Joint that articulates quadrate and hyomandibula bones. Quadrate-hyomandibula joint is paired.
Joint that articulates quadrate and metapterygoid bones. Quadrate-metapterygoid joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the ventral hypohyal and urohyal bones. Ventral hypohyal-urohyal joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the left and right dentary bones. Mandibular symphysis is unpaired.
Is a portion of tissue part of the heart primordium which will give rise to the endocardium.
Portion of tissue that will develop into the myocardium.
Groove that is in the ventral optic cup remnant of the optic fissure.
Fossa where the hyomandibula articulates with the sphenotic and prootic to form the hyomandibular-otic region joint.
Organ entity that is typically involved in mechanical support and may have different skeletal tissue compositions at different stages.
Surface structure that is a portion of skin that projects or hangs from the body.
Surface structure that is the region of the head in front of and between the orbits, and above the lower jaw. Nares are located on the snout.
Joint that articulates the epihyal and ceratohyal bones. Epihyal-ceratohyal joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the epihyal and branchiostegal ray bones. Epihyal-branchiostegal ray joint is paired.
Fine ridges on surface of scale, often parallel to the circumference of scale.
Is a bony partially gas filled sphere located ventral and anterior to the inner ear.  The prootic bulla is paired.
Interopercular-mandibular ligament is a ligament that connects the interopercle to the retroarticular element of the lower jaw.

comments: The interopercle ossifies in the interoperculomandibular ligament, which attaches to the retroarticular. The interopercle approximates and is ligamentously attached to the retroarticular anteroventrally. ZDB-PUB-961014-192.  Need develops_in relationship for interopercle.

definition.val: Lateral ethmoid-autopalatine ligament is a ligament that connects the lateral ethmoid to the autopalatine.
Lateral ethmoid-ectopterygoid ligament is a ligament that connects the lateral ethmoid to the ectopterygoid.
Joint between the centra, and sometimes the neural arches or parapophyses of vertebra 4 and vertebra 5.
Joint between the centra, and sometimes the neural arches or parapophyses.
Joint between the centra, and sometimes the neural arches or parapophyses of vertebra 6 and vertebra 7.
Multi-tissue structure composed of connective tissue that surrounds, the internal portions of the olfactory epithelium.

Mental barbel that originates on the chin, in an anteromedial position relative to the outer mental barbel. The inner mental barbel is nearer to the mandibular symphysis than is the outer mental barbel.
Anatomical cluster that is the upper roof of the mouth that is roofed by bones such as the parasphenoid, prevomer, metapterygoid, orbitosphenoid.
Shallow area that exists on the prootic.
Dermal bone that is ring-shaped or cylindrical and surrounds a sensory canal.
Caudal principal ray that is the dorsalmost caudal principal ray of the caudal fin.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 2.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 1.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 3.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 4.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 5.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 6.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 7.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 8.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 9.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 10.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 11.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 13.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 14.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 15.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 16.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 17.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 18.
Scale that is associated with the lateral line sensory canal.
The bony shelf above orbit is an anatomical cluster formed by the dorsolateral portions of the lateral ethmoid, frontal and sphenotic bones that forms the dorsal margin of the orbit.

Dermal bone that is located on or subjacent to the surface anywhere on the body and that is not part of the general complement of scales, dermal bone of the dermatocranium, postcranial axial skeleton or paired fin skeleton. Bony plate may exist singly, or as unpaired or paired clusters or series.
Caudal fin lepidotrichium that is distally branched.
Dorsal fin ray that is distally branched.
Anal fin lepidotrichium that is distally branched.
Anatomical space between bones of the neurocranium.  Frequently closes during development.
Fontanel within the frontal bone near the frontal-sphenotic joint. Lateral fontanel of frontal is paired.
Rib that articulates with the parapophysis of the first centrum.
Rib that articulates with the parapophysis of the second centrum.
Rib that articulates with the parapophysis of the third centrum.
Rib that articulates with the parapophysis of the fourth centrum.
Rib that articulates with the parapophysis of the fifth centrum.
Rib that articulates with the parapophysis of the sixth centrum.
Joint that articulates the hyomandibular cartilage or bone and otic region cartilage or sphenotic bone, and often pterotic bone and prootic bone. Hyomandibular-otic region joint is paired.
Actinotrichium that is part of the pectoral fin.
Actinotrichium that is part of the pelvic fin.
Pectoral fin lepidotrichium 1 is the leading or marginal ray of the fin and articulates with the scapula.  Lepidotrichium 1 is commonly segmented but unbranched.
Pectoral fin lepidotrichium that, in a linear sequence, is the second most anterior pectoral ray, usually branched and segmented.
Pectoral fin lepidotrichium that, in a linear sequence, is the third most anterior pectoral ray, usually branched and segmented.
Pectoral fin lepidotrichium that, in a linear sequence, is the sixth most anterior pectoral ray, usually branched and segmented.
Pectoral fin lepidotrichium that, in a linear sequence, is the seventh most anterior pectoral ray, usually branched and segmented.
Pectoral fin lepidotrichium that, in a linear sequence, is the fifth most anterior pectoral ray , usually branched and segmented.
Pectoral fin lepidotrichium that, in a linear sequence, is the fourth most anterior pectoral ray, usually branched and segmented.
First lepidotrichium of pelvic fin.
Second lepidotrichium of pelvic fin.
Fourth lepidotrichium of pelvic fin.
Third lepidotrichium of pelvic fin.
Anatomical space that is the concavity containing the eye.
Portion of vessel wall that extends to form a new vessel.
Joint that articulates the parasphenoid and basioccipital. Parasphenoid-basioccipital joint is paired.
Portion of organism substance that is a gelatinous extracellular matrix structure that fills the vitreous cavity of the eye.
Acellular anatomical structure that is the innermost layer of the optic choroid.
Multi-tissue structure that composes the opaque fibrous outer layer of the eye.
Clear ocular fluid in the anterior segment of the eye.  It is produced primarily by dorsal ciliary epithelial cells.
A specialized hair cell that has an elongated kinocilium upon which an otolith accretes. The tether cell then anchors the otolith in place.
Anatomical cluster comprising the rostral portion of the eye;  the cornea, lens, iris, ciliary zone, anterior chamber, and annular ligament.
Anatomical cluster comprising the caudal portion of the eye: the retina, posterior chamber, optic choroid, and vitreous.
Blood vessels within the stroma of the iris.  The vessels cluster  and are more dense at the base of the stroma where the iris is thicker.

Portion of the iris which contains  bundles of fibers, most of which are oriented radially around the pupil. The stroma is innervated by the iris blood vessels and develops from the periocular mesenchyme.
The line where the cornea and the sclera meet.
Duct present in the angle where the cornea meets the ciliary zone that absorbs the aqueous humor.
Head mesenchyme surrounding the immature eye that  gives rise to specialized structures of the anterior segment of the eye.

Modified or toughened vitreous that suspend the lens at the equator to the non-pigmented epithelium of the ciliary zone.  In zebrafish, dorsal zonules are thickened as compared to ventral zonules. In both dorsal and ventral regions, these fibers delimit the vitreous body aqueous humor boundary.
The lens capsule is a modified basement membrane that completely surrounds the ocular lens.
Portion of tissue that are compact accumulations of neurons having roughly similar connections and functions.
Brain nuclei located in the ventral thalamus.
The multi-tissue structure that is the beginning of the spinal nerve leaving the spinal cord.
Integument ionocyte rich in Na-Cl co-transporter (NCC) activity.
Rostral portion of pars anterior of the adenohypophysis where corticotropes and lactotropes localize.

Caudal portion of pars anterior of the adenohypophysis where somatotropes, thyrotropes and gonadotropes are localized.  The proximal pars anterior has functional homology to the mammalian pars tuberalis, secreting the same hormones.
Cluster of cells along the edge of  the neural plate that have the potential to become neuroblast.
Cluster of cells along the center of  the neural plate that have the potential to become neuroblast.
Anatomical cluster which give rise to mature mesonephric nephrons.  Zebrafish continously generate new mesonephric nephrons.
Renal duct that will develop into the mesonephric proximal tubule.
Hollow cluster of epithelial cells derived from the nephron progenitor.
Cluster of cells comprising a portion of tissue which gives rise to new mesonephric nephrons.  The cluster contains self-renewing stem cells.
Developing nephron with a comma shaped appearance.
Developing nephron with a tubular morphology that has a renal corpuscle anlage at the distal end.
Developing nephron where the developing glomerular capsule has been invaded by a glomerular capillary loop called the glomerular tuft.
Renal duct that will develop into the mesonephric distal tubule.
A branched nephron of the mature kidney.
An anatomical structure that is regional part of the central nervous system where there is a significant bend of the axis.
Non-material anatomical entity of two dimensions, that is demarcated by anatomical lines or points on the external or internal surfaces of anatomical structures.
Caudal principal ray that is ventrally adjacent to caudal principal ray 12.
Fin that is one of a pair located ventrally on the organism.
Fin that is an unpaired fin located in the sagittal plane of the organism.
Ghrelin secreting cells found in the found in the exocrine pancreas.

comments: In mammals the endocrine pancreas is called the Islets of Langerhans.

definition.val: A cell that secretes ghrelin, the peptide hormone that stimulates hunger.
Region at venious pole of heart tube that initiates heart beat.
Ring of cardiac conduction tissue around the sinoatrial region of the heart tube that performs pacemaker function.
Supraneural located posterior to supraneural 9.  This supraneural does not appear in most specimens.
Portion of vessel wall that extends to form a new lymph vessel.
Portion of vessel wall that extends to form a new blood vessel.

The primordial vasculature that will develop into lymph vessels.  The lymphangioblast cord is composed of lymphangioblast or lymph endothelial cells in a solid linear mass called a cord.  The cord then undergoes morphogenesis to form the lumen of the vessel.
Anatomical cluster of skeletal elements that forms the roof over the brain.
Fibrous joint connecting the parietal bones.
Fibrous joint connecting the frontal bone to the parietal bone.  This joint remains patent in zebrafish throughout their lifetime.
Fibrous joint connecting the supraoccipital to the parietal bone.
Joint in which the articulating bones are connected by bone.
Synostosis where the entopterygoid meets the quadrate.
Synostosis where the ectopterygoid meets the entopterygoid.
Synchondrosis where the basioccipital meets the prootic anteriorly.
Synostosis where the entopterygoid meets the metapterygoid. The metapterygoid has an anterodorsally projecting spine that meets the entopterygoid medially in a synostosis at its posteromedial edge.
Opening formed between the ventral border of the metapterygoid and the dorsal border of the symplectic.
Synchondrosis where the quadrate meets the entopterygoid dorsally.
Synostosis where anteromedial extensions of the lateral ethmoid project to meet the supraethmoid dorsally.
Cartilaginous joint that articulates the anguloarticular posteroventrally with the retroarticular.
Connective tissue that functions in mechanical and structural support.
A skeletal element that has the potential to participate in endochondral ossification, and may participate in intramembranous ossification.

Skeletal tissue with a collagen-rich extracellular matrix vascularized, mineralized with hydroxyapatite and typically including osteocytes located in lacunae that communicate with one another by cell processes (in canaliculi). Bone is deposited by osteoblasts.
Skeletal tissue that is avascular, rich in glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) and typically includes chondrocytes within isolated lacunae. Cartilage tissue is deposited by chondroblasts.
Skeletal tissue that is part of the exoskeleton and derived from an odontogenic papilla.
Skeletal element that forms as a replacement or substitution of another element or tissue.
Space enclosed by the trabeculae cranii.  The anterior tip of the notochord extends into the posterior region of the hypophyseal fenestra.
Synovial joint that articulates the hyomandibula and the opercle.
Synovial joint that articulates the hyomandibula with the pterotic.
Fossa where the exoccipital forms the medial portion and the epioccipital forms the lateral margin.

Foramen in the hyomandibula that allows passage of the anterior lateral line nerve and the facial nerve (cranial nerve VII). The foramen develop in the hyosymplectic.  The hyomandibular foramen are paired.
Fossa on the pterotic  where the hyomandibula articulates.
Fibrous joint that articulates the posterior edge of the  preopercle horizontal limb with the symplectic.
Fibrous joint that articulates the anterior edge of the preopercle vertical limb and the  hyomandibula.
Skeletal element which is part of lepidotrichium articulating with other lepidotrichium segments by lepidotrichium joints.
Joint that articulates segments of the fin ray.

Odontoid tissue formed of a loose matrix of collagen.  The fibrils of the predentine are homogeneously distributed and preferentially oriented along the long axis of the tooth. They are of variable thickness, measuring from 50 to 100 nm in diameter.
Ligament that connects the intercalarium to the  anterior process of the tripus and the scaphium.
Ligament that connects one rib to another.
Ligaments that runs from the parapophyses of the anteriormost centrum to the exoccipital.  There are two  scapulo-vertebral ligaments in zebrafish.
Ligament that runs from the ventral third of the supracleithrum to the exoccipital.
Ligament that run from the dorsal tip of the cleithrum to the exoccipital.
Ligament that connects the dentary to the maxilla.
Ligament that connects the  rostral process of the maxilla  to the kinethmoid.
Ligament that, in the adult, attaches the premaxilla to its counterpart at the midline and the kinethmoid posterodorsally.
The ligament that connects the anteroventral tips of ceratobranchials 5 medially.
Short ligament that runs from the hooked anterior end of the supracleithrum to the intercalar.
Ligament that connects the  preopercle to the retroarticular anteroventrally.
Dense regular connective tissue that connects muscle to bone.
Joint that articulates the mesethmoid and lateral ethmoid. Mesethmoid-lateral ethmoid joint is paired.
Joint that articulates the mesethmoid and vomer. Mesethmoid-vomer joint is unpaired.

Adductor that originates at the cleithrum the muscle fibers run in sheets along the endoskeletal disk extending dorsally and caudally from its base. Distally, the muscles insert onto the fin membrane.

Abductor that originates at the cleithrum the muscle fibers run in sheets along the endoskeletal disk extending dorsally and caudally from its base. Distally, the muscles insert onto the fin membrane.
Layers of broad flat tendons.
Portion of tissue in the vagal lobe that contains GABAergic sensory interneurons.
Portion of organism substance that is the liquid matrix of blood.
Endochondral process of the autopalatine that forms the socket of the autopalatine-preethmoid joint.
Synovial, ball and socket joint that articulates the autopalatine and the preethmoid.
Endochondral bone part of autopalatine that extends towards the first infraorbital.
Portion of organism substance found in the lymphatic system.  Lymph usually consists of interstitial fluid that has made its way into the lymphatic system.
Thin layer of loose connective tissue which lies beneath the intestinal  epithelium. The intestinal lamina propria is less complex then the corresponding mammalian tissue.
Multi-tissue structure of the intestine comprising of the intestinal epithelium and the intestinal lamina propria mucosa.

Multi-tissue structure part of the olfactory bulb  that  is roughly spheroidal and encircled by olfactory sensory neuron axons that formed an anatomically distinct stalk  connected to the olfactory nerve.
Bilaterally-paired nerve connecting each segment of the spinal cord to the limbs and organs.

Dorsal branch of the spinal nerve, coursing dorsally and caudally between the muscle and the vertebrae. It remains in the center of the muscle segment and could be followed to the dorsal aspect of the muscle. MiP primary motoneuron axons extend in this branch.

Ventral branch of the spinal nerve that projects toward the lateral surface of the myotome, initially extending parallel to the horizontal myoseptum. RoP primary motoneuron axons extend in this branch.
Ventral spinal nerve branch that projects to the ventral myotome. The axon of the CaP primary motoneuron extends in this branch.
Ventral spinal nerve sub-branch that runs near the rostral end of the myotome near the vertical myoseptum.
Ventral spinal nerve sub-branch that projects ventrally in the rostro-caudal center of the myotome. The axon of the CaP primary motoneuron extends in this branch.

Multi-tissue structure part of the olfactory bulb comprised of ciliated and microvillous olfactory sensory neurons projections that form specialized neuropilar regions comprised of olfactory sensor axons and the dendrites of olfactory bulb neurons.
Protoglomeruli in the lateral  region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.  The neurons composing lateral protoglomeruli respond to amino acid stimuli.
Protoglomeruli in the central zone  of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.  The central zone protoglomeruli have somewhat indistinct boundaries.
Protoglomeruli in the medial  region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb. Mediates the signals produced in response to bile acids.
Single large glomerulus located in the ventral posterior region of the maturing glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.
Central region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.
Dorsal region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.

Lateral region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.  The lateral region of the olfactory bulb consisted of morphologically diverse glomeruli and a diffuse aggregate of OSN axons, the lateral plexus.
Medial region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.
Ventral region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.
Lateral region of the dorsal zone of the olfactory bulb. Glomeruli in this zone are tightly clustered in both male and female fish.
Medial portion of the ventral region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.

Anterior portion of the ventral region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb. In the ventroanterior zone there are  typically seven small glomeruli located 11-20 micrometers beneath the superficial axon bundles  The ganglion differed conspicuously in sizes, shapes, and distribution, and were not individually  identifiable.
Posterior portion of the ventral region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.

nodes.lbl: mediodorsal zone of the olfactory bulb

definition.val: Dorsal part of the medial region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.
Anterior portion of the medial region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.
Glomerulus located between ventromedial and medial olfactory bulb surfaces. The mpG is generally  elongated and innervated by a single thick axon.
Glomerulus located in the dorsal zone of the olfactory bulb.  It is located in a stereotypic position near the posterior edge of the dorsal glomerular zone and stains with anti-calretinin.
Glomerulus located in the dorsolateral zone of the olfactory bulb.  Dorsalmost of glomeruli located along the caudal edge of the olfactory bulb.
Glomerulus located in the dorsolateral zone of the olfactory bulb.  Ventral to dlG1 and located along the caudal edge of the olfactory bulb.
Glomerulus located in the dorsolateral zone of the olfactory bulb.  Ventral to dlG2 and located along the caudal edge of the olfactory bulb.
Glomerulus located in the dorsolateral zone of the olfactory bulb.  Ventral to dlG3 and located along the caudal edge of the olfactory bulb.
Glomerulus located in the dorsolateral zone of the olfactory bulb.  Ventral to dlG4,  and located along the caudal edge of the olfactory bulb.
Glomerulus located in the ventromedial zone of the olfactory bulb. Stains strongly with anti-calretinin. Anteriormost of the triplet of glomeruli strongly expressing calretinin.
Glomerulus located in the ventromedial zone of the olfactory bulb. Stains strongly with anti-calretinin.  Posterior to vmG1 in the triplet of glomeruli strongly expressing calretinin.
Glomerulus located in the ventromedial zone of the olfactory bulb. Stains strongly with anti-calretinin.  Posteriormost glomerulus in the triplet of glomeruli strongly expressing calretinin.
Glomerulus located in the ventromedial zone of the olfactory bulb. Posterior to the triplet of  ventromedial glomeruli strongly expressing calretinin.
Glomerulus located in the ventromedial zone of the olfactory bulb. Posterior to vmG4.
Glomerulus located in the ventromedial zone of the olfactory bulb. Posterior to vmG4 adjacent to vmG5.
Glomerulus located in the ventromedial zone of the olfactory bulb. vmG7 is located anteriorly to vmG1-3 and vmG4-6.

Group of glomerulus located in the ventromedial zone of the olfactory bulb The vmGx group, is  located 10-50 micrometers from the ventral surface of the olfactory bulb. And were located anywhere between the posteroventral and anteroventral regions of the olfactory bulb. It was not possible to unambiguously identify the vmGx because they displayed varying distributions and numbers in the olfactory bulbs of different animals.

Group of glomerulus located in the ventromedial zone of the olfactory bulb. A cluster of comparatively small glomeruli located near the midline between the two olfactory bulbs The glomeruli in the  vmGy were among the smallest glomeruli in the olfactory bulb.
One of two large glomeruli located in the medial side of the ventroposterior zone olfactory bulb.
One of two large glomeruli located in the lateral side of the ventroposterior zone olfactory bulb.
Glomerulus located in the lateral zone of the olfactory bulb. LG1 is surrounded by a diffuse aggregate of OSN axons.
Glomerulus located in the lateral zone of the olfactory bulb. Located on the caudal edge of the lateral glomerulus.
Glomerulus located in the dorsoanterior region of the  lateral zone of the olfactory bulb. Expresses calretinin.
Glomerulus located in the lateral zone of the olfactory bulb.
Glomerulus located in the lateral zone of the olfactory bulb. LG5 is surrounded by a diffuse aggregate of OSN axons.

Group of glomerulus located in the lateral region of the olfactory bulb. The  glomeruli that compose lGx are smaller than the lG1-6 and varied in shape, sizes, and locations between olfactory bulbs of the same and different animals. The lGx were located either along the ventral edge of the lateral group or below the lateral olfactory bulb surface.
Glomerulus located in the lateral zone of the olfactory bulb. LG6 is located on the ventroposterior edge of the lateral group.
Glomerulus located in the mediodorsal zone of the olfactory bulb. It lies caudal to mdG5 and mdG6 and rostral of mdG3.
Glomerulus located in the mediodorsal zone of the olfactory bulb.  This glomerulus expresses a S100 protein.  It is the most posterior-caudal of the mediodorsal glomeruli.
Glomerulus located in the mediodorsal zone of the olfactory bulb.  Glomerulus that lies ventral to mdG2 on the caudal edge of the mediodorsal glomeruli.
Glomerulus located in the mediodorsal zone of the olfactory bulb.  Positioned between mdG2 and mdG5 on the dorsal edge of the medial dorsal glomerular cluster.

Glomerulus located in the mediodorsal zone of the olfactory bulb. .  This glomerulus expresses a protein that cross reacts with antibodies raised against GNAO1.  It is the  dorsal to mdG6 on the rostral edge of the mediodorsal cluster.
Glomerulus located in the mediodorsal zone of the olfactory bulb.  This glomerulus is the most ventral glomerulus in the mediodorsal cluster.
Glomerulus located in the anteromedial  zone of the olfactory bulb.
Group of small glomeruli located in the anteromedial zone of the olfactory bulb. The arrangements of glomeruli maGx varies between animals and they can't be identified as individual units.
Protoglomeruli in the lateral  region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb. Dorsolateral protoglomerulus located in the lateral region of the olfactory bulb.
Protoglomeruli in the lateral  region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb. Ventromedial protoglomerulus located in the lateral region of the olfactory bulb.
Protoglomeruli in the lateral  region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb. Dorsomedial protoglomerulus located in the lateral region of the olfactory bulb.
Protoglomeruli in the lateral  region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb. Ventrolateral protoglomerulus located in the lateral region of the olfactory bulb.

Protoglomeruli in the central zone  of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.  The central zone protoglomeruli have somewhat indistinct boundaries.  Most lateral protoglomerulus in the central zone.
Protoglomeruli in the central zone  of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.  The central zone protoglomeruli have somewhat indistinct boundaries.  Located medially to protoglomerulus 1.
Protoglomeruli in the central zone  of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.  The central zone protoglomeruli have somewhat indistinct boundaries.  Located medially to protoglomerulus 2.
Protoglomeruli in the central zone  of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.  The central zone protoglomeruli have somewhat indistinct boundaries.   Located medially to protoglomerulus 3.

Protoglomeruli in the central zone  of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.  The central zone protoglomeruli have somewhat indistinct boundaries. Most medial protoglomerulus in the central zone.
Protoglomeruli in the medial  region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.  Most posterio-lateral of the medial protoglomeruli.
Protoglomeruli in the medial  region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.  Most dorsally located medial protoglomerulus and medial to protoglomerulus 1.

Protoglomeruli in the medial  region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb.  Located  between medial glomerulus 2 and medial glomerulus 4 along dorsal-ventral axis of the medial region of the olfactory bulb.
Protoglomeruli in the medial  region of the glomerular layer of the olfactory bulb. Most ventral  of the medial protoglomeruli.
Motor neuron that innervate muscles that control eye, jaw, and facial movements of the vertebrate head and parasympathetic neurons that innervate certain glands and organs.
Cranial motor neuron which innervates muscles derived from the branchial (pharyngeal) arches.

Cranial motor neurons are found in the hindbrain, and innervate parasympathetic neurons that innervate tear glands, sweat glands, and the smooth muscles and glands of the pulmonary, cardiovascular, and gastrointestinal systems.
Cranial motor neurons found in the midbrain and hindbrain that innervates muscles that control eye and tongue.

A system of osteocytes connected through canals within the vertebra.  Osteocytes extend processes through canals within ossified bone.  The lacunocanalicular system is poorly developed in zebrafish with most  osteocytes having one or two cytoplasmic processes.
Anatomical space, part of the lacunocanalicular system that connects lacuna within bone tissue allowing osteocyte projections to connect.
Anatomical space a small cavity, in the matrix of bone tissue. Part of the lacunocanalicular system.

Multicellular structure composed of endodermal cells that forms a continuous rod along the anterior-posterior axis of the embryo in contact with the YSL.  This rod will give rise to the endodermal components of the alimentary canal and its connected organs: liver, gall bladder pancreas and swim bladder. Does not have part pharyngeal endoderm.

Portion of the intestinal rod that loops to left between 26-30hpf and gives rise to the intestinal bulb and liver and anterior pancreatic primordium.  The endoderm at this stage is a compact mass of cells with little to no polarization.

A secretory cell found in pancreatic acini that secretes digestive enzymes and mucins. This cell is a typical zymogenic cell, has a basal nucleus and basophilic cytoplasm consisting of regular arrays of granular endoplasmic reticulum with mitochondria and dense secretory granules.

A cuboidal epithelial cell that is continuous with the lining of intercalated ducts that drain the acinus. This cell type secretes a high pH solution to aid in activation of zymogens, and can differentiate into endocrine and exocrine pancreatic cell types.

Portion of exocrine pancreas that is comprised of polarized cells containing apical zymogen granules that store digestive enzymes such as trypsin and chymotrypsin and abundant rough endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria.
Polypeptide producing cell located in the islets of the pancreas.
A D cell located in the pancreas. Peripherally placed within the islets like type A cells; contains somatostatin.
Cells that  form an epithelial-like sheath around the vacuolated inner notochord cells. Outer cells secrete a thick extracellular perinotochordal basement membrane.
A cell whose primary function is to provide structural support, to provide strength and physical integrity to the organism.
Structural cell of notochord that contain one large fluid-filled intracellular vacuole that occupies most of the cell volume.
Lymph vessels that are located on either side of the PCV in the posterior region of the intestine.
Digestive duct within the pancreas.  The pancreatic duct carries inactive digestive enzymes to the intestine.
The portion of the intrapancreatic duct leading from the pancreatic acinar gland to the intralobular duct.
Intrapancreatic duct within a pancreatic lobe that connects all the  intercalated duct with an interlobular duct.  The intralobular duct is lined with cuboidal cells.
Intrapancreatic duct that connects the intralobular duct to the main pancreatic duct.
Intrapancreatic duct that connects to the interlobular ducts and drains into the extrapancreatic duct.  The main duct may not be present in all larva and may develop in a segmented fashion.
Cluster of acinar glands surrounding an intralobular duct.

comments: Lobules are composed of multiple acini so are only apparent starting ~72hpf.  Before 72hpf consider annotating to acinar gland.

definition.val: The primary islet is the first islet to develop.
Portion of tissue composed of a group of cells in the pancreas producing insulin, glucagon and somatostatin. Part of the endocrine pancreas.
Islets that develop after the primary islet.  The secondary islets usually form around 7 dpf but can be induced to form as early as 5 dpf.

comments: Start stage known to be after 3dpf.

definition.val: Portion of tissue composed of the signal-generating cells within the zona limitans intrathalamica.
Adipose tissue interspersed within the pancreatic acini.

comments: No evidence of septa surrounding fat has been presented so is a fat accumulation or deposit not a fat pad.

definition.val: Adipose tissue on the surface of the organs within the pleuroperitoneal cavity. Visceral adipocytes were observed posterior to the swim bladder and lining the visceral cavity.

comments: No evidence of septa surrounding fat has been presented so is a fat accumulation or deposit not a fat pad.

definition.val: Adipose tissue in the cranium. Cranial adipocytes are located within the ventral wall of the cranium, situated between the brain and the esophagus.

comments: No evidence of septa surrounding fat has been presented so is a fat accumulation or deposit not a fat pad.

definition.val: Adipose tissue surrounding the eye.

comments: No evidence of septa surrounding fat has been presented so is a fat accumulation or deposit not a fat pad.

definition.val: Adipose tissue between muscle and integument.
Adipose tissue surrounding the esophagus.
Adipose tissue deposited along the mandible.
Adipose tissue surrounding the heart.
Adipose tissue that forms at the base of the pectoral fin.
Subcutaneous adipose tissue deposit at the base of the caudal fin.

Sensory neuron that extends a single axon shaft along the lateral white matter of the hindbrain and spinal cord. Numerous medially projecting branches are later formed and innervate a series of target nuclei along the anteroposterior axis of the hindbrain and spinal cord.  The highly branched axons innervate the integument of the head.  There are several subtypes with different afferent branching morphologies.
A male germ cell that develops from the haploid secondary spermatocytes. Without further division, spermatids undergo structural changes and give rise to spermatozoa.

Specialized olfactory support cell that surrounds crypt olfactory sensory neurons.   There are one or two support cells for each crypt neuron.  The cytoplasm of crypt neuron olfactory support cells is less electron dense then other support cells.  They also bear numerous microvilli at their apical rim. When two supporting cells are present, they interdigitate with each other  Mitochondria in crypt supporting cells are larger than those in the common supporting cells.
Portion of tissue that develops from the cranial neural crest and which cells populate a series of pharyngeal arches from which develops the support skeleton of the jaw and gills.
Diploid cell produced by the fusion of sperm cell nucleus and egg cell.

nodes.lbl: embryonic blood vessel endothelial progenitor cell

definition.val: An endothelial progenitor cell that participates in angiogenesis during development.
The external layer of the atrium bounding the atrial myocardium.
Neuron that is part of the enteric nervous system.
Parasympathetic neurons are part of the parasympathetic nervous system and the cell bodies lie in the brain and sacral region of the spinal cord. The neurons are mainly cholinergic.
Sympathetic neurons are part of the sympathetic nervous system and are primarily adrenergic producing the neurotransmitter noradrenalin along with other neuropeptides.
A neuron that releases hypocretin as a neurotransmitter.

Epithelium within the thymus where lymphocytes mature.  Cells of the thymic epithelium have cytoplasm that appear devoid of most membrane bound organelles but contain bundles of cytoplasmic filaments, and frequent desmosomes occur between these epithelial cells. There are also secretory epithelial cells that contain a  heterogeneous population of electron-dense, cytoplasmic granules and a well developed Golgi complex.   Epithelial cysts are present after 3 weeks.

Epithelium of the thymus primordium composed of cells of immature morphology. The immature thymic epithelium is continuous with the pharyngeal epithelium.  The immature thymic epithelium is composed of electron-lucent epithelial cells of irregular or polygonal shape and contain numerous ribosomes, large electron-lucent mitochondria, and rough endoplasmic reticulum. Nascent desmosomes join these primitive epithelial cells together.
Part of the gut where the pronephric tubules come together with the caudal end of the intestine attaching at 4dpf.  The oviduct or sperm duct connect later.
The juncture where the  intestine empties into the cloaca.
Epithelium lining the cloaca.  The epithelium is a simple cuboidal epithelium at Prim-5.
Muscle cell which has as its direct parts myofilaments organized into sarcomeres.
Striated muscle of the esophagus. Permits voluntary swallowing.

Wedge-shaped epidermal cell positioned along the apex of the developing fin fold. Each cleft cell retains a wedge-shaped form during fold formation and develops a basal cleft-shaped invagination. Invaginations are aligned in neighboring cleft cells so that these cells cap the distal boundary of the subepidermal space where collagenous extracellular fibres called actinotrichia run anteroposteriorly along the length of the fin fold.

Artery that runs along the midline from anterior to posterior regions of the hypothalamus and splits into two capillary branches (hyc). The formation of the artery is initiated at the hypophysis itself and extends anteriorly until it connects with the palatocerebral arteries at day 2.5.
Vein thats tarts posterior of the hypophysis and drains the hypophyseal capillaries.  It empties into the primary head sinus.

Axon tract originating  from the two hemispheres of the neurosecretory preoptic areas  converging into the midline and terminating at the ventral part of the hypothalamus at the presumed location of the pituitary. Analogous to the supraopticohypophyseal tract in mammals.

Pair of capillaries that bilaterally encompass the hypophysis starting at the anterior aspect of the hypophysis (pituitary).   Directly posterior to the pituitary, the two capillaries fuse with the hypophyseal vein.
Surface structure that is a keratinized epidermal appendages that is a contact organ and secondary sex organ.
Surface structure that is a protrusion from the rostral end of the jaw, supported by the mandibular projection.

Layer of smooth cells composing the outer layer of the breeding tubercle.  Cells in this layer have a high keratin content, lamellar bodies, and condensed chromosomes.  This layer is repeatedly shed and regenerated.

nodes.lbl: second tier layer of breeding tubercle

definition.val: Cell layer that lies underneath the superficial layer of the breeding tubercle.  Characterized by tight junctions within the layer.
Intermediate layer of the breeding tubercles characterized by cells with  loose spinous organization connected to each other via desmosomes, but with wide extracellular spaces in between.
Layer of breeding tubercle proximal to basal lamina of the integument.
Breeding tubercles along the surface of the pectoral fin lepidotrichium of males.
Surface structures composed of breeding tubercles arranged in a double row in the medial region on each side of the lower jaw.
Breeding tubercles that cover the surface of the jaw flap.
Portion of organism substance that is located between the cap and second tier layers of the breeding tubercles.  The lipid lamellae is composed of lipids.

The interrenal vessel (IRV) is the blood vessels supplying the interrenal gland. It is formed by sprouting from the dorsal aorta.  The development of the IRV is temporally and spatially correlated with the initiation interrenal gland formation.  After traversing the interrenal tissue, the IRV connects  to the posterior end segment of the anterior mesenteric artery where two branches of swim bladder arteries branched out.  Angiogenic sprout is visible between 34 and 48 hpf with blood flow by 3dpf.
Sprout from the dorsal aorta that develops into the interrenal vessel.
Gelatinous substance surrounding the kinocila of the neuromast.

nodes.lbl: rhombencephalic efferent neurons to the lateral line

definition.val: Efferent neuron where the cell body is located in the hindbrain reticular formation immediately adjacent to the medial longitudinal fascicle at the rostrocaudal level between the MiDl and the CaD and adjacent to the descending Mauthner axon.  RELL dendrites are large and extend bilaterally into the ventrolateral neuropil.  The ipsilateral dendrite reaches into a single area of the ventrolateral neuropil whereas the contralateral dendrite branches and extends into two different levels of the same rostrocaudal column of the ventrolateral neuropil. The axon emerges from the ventral part of the perikaryon courses a short distance rostrally to the level of the ROLE  cells and then turns laterally from the CNS accompanying the root of the posterior lateral line nerve.

Efferent neuron located in the hindbrain.  The axon of ROLE cells travels rostrally near the ipsilateral medial longitudinal fascicle to the level of the Mauthner cell where it turns laterally to exit with the eighth nerve.  Outside the brain the  axon continues ventrally  to the base of the saccular macula in the ear then turns caudally to ender the ganglion of the posterior lateral line.   A branch of the axon  turns ventrally before the main axon enters the LLG.  The branch goes ventrally along the wall of the otic capsule.

nodes.lbl: diencephalic efferent neurons to the lateral line

definition.val: Efferent neurons located ventrally in the diencephalon.  The dendrites extend into a large area of the diencephalic neuropil rostral, caudal, lateral and ventral to the cell body.  The axon courses dorsally and then caudally through the midbrain.   As the axon descends through the brain it gives off branches at several levels.   One branch usually exits the brain with the posterior lateral line nerve.  The axon can be traced into the cervical spinal cord.

comments: There may be 2 neuron types grouped under this name since there are some instances the axons were seen to exit with the eighth cranial nerve instead of the PLL nerve:  ZDB-PUB-961014-768.

definition.val: Anatomical space within the intestine where digestion normally occurs.  The intestinal lumen begins as an interconnected web of small space at 43 hpf that over time remodels to form a single continuous tube at 68hpf.  Lumen maturation proceeds from anterior to posterior along the intestine.

Transient cluster of cardiac progenitor cells that develops adjacent to the heart and migrates onto the heart to form the epicardium.  Proepicardial clusters in Zebrafish form on the pericardial wall, adjacent to the atrioventricular (AV) junction and near the venous pole.

comments: Appears after cardiac looping ~30hpf. Derived from mesoderm.

definition.val: Network of heavily branched muscle in the atrium.
Trabecular layer of the atrial myocardium.
Is an interconnected tubular multi-tissue structure that contains fluid that is actively transported around the heart.
Artery that carries blood from the efferent branchial artery to the cardiac ventricle.  The artery runs along the ventral surface of the bulbus arteriosus.
Capillaries that innervate the subtrabecular layer of the heart.
Vein that carries blood from the coronary capillaries to the atrial chamber entering the atrium close to the atrioventricular region.

TARGET DEFINITION: Hindbrain interneurons that are part of the reticular formation.  The T interneurons receive synaptic input from the  Mauthner neuron,  from unidentified axons in the mlf, and perhaps from trigeminal sensory fibers.  .  The axon is T-shaped the proximal segment courses to the midline of the  brain and near the midline bifurcates to form an ascending rostral segment and a descending caudal segment.  The rostral and caudal segments join the dorsal component of the MLF.  The T interneurons are located caudal to CaD and appear to be bilaterally paired. They project to cranial and pectoral motor nuclei.

comments: This term is a leaf node in the zebrafish AO and is not in the CL.

definition.val: Anterior longitudinal muscles of the dorsal fin.  The dorsal fin protractor  originates from the base of the first soft ray of the dorsal fin, extends along the midline just underneath the skin, and inserts at the posterior portion of the skull.  Contracting the  dorsal protractor will raise the fin.

Anterior longitudinal muscles of the dorsal fin.  That originates from the caudal end of the dorsal fin base, stretches along the midline between the right and left epaxial muscles, and inserts at the dorsal base of the tail fin.  Contracting the dorsal retractor will lower the fin.
The layer of caudal fin musculature immediately under the integument that originate from the vertebral column and insert at the base of caudal fin rays.

The layer of caudal fin musculature immediately under the superficial caudal fin musculature that originate from the vertebral column and insert at the base of caudal fin rays.  The three most caudal vertebrae and the hypurals serve as attachment sites for these muscles.
Paired, deep caudal fin muscles that arise broadly from the hypurals and extends dorsocaudally where the fiber bundles converge into a narrow insertion site on the 5th caudal fin ray.

Paired superficial caudal fin muscle that has a narrow attachment site along the  midline posterior to the attachment of the lateralis superficialis ventralis  and fans out to attach broadly in a region between the 12th and 16th caudal fin ray.

Paired superficial caudal fin muscle that has a narrow attachment site along the midline posterior to the attachment of the lateralis superficialis dorsalis  and fans out to attach broadly in a region between the 4th and 8th caudal fin ray.
Paired superficial caudal fin muscle that originates from a broad base at the midline and narrows toward its attachment at the dorsal tip of the caudal fin.

Paired superficial caudal fin muscle that is located ventrally and extends from the myoseptum to the ventral tip of the caudal fin where it attaches in the region of the second lowest (eighteenth) long fin ray.
Paired superfical caudal fin muscle that is located dorsally and extends from the myoseptum to the dorsal tip of the caudal fin where it attaches in the region of the second upper long fin ray.

Cardiac muscle  located in the trabecular layer of the ventricle that is attached to the base of the AV and BV valves.  It is similar to the papillary muscle in mammals and birds but,  does not attach to the ends of the valve leaflets.

Cardiac muscle  located in the trabecular layer of the ventricle that spans the central lumen near the ventricular apex.  The transverse muscle band superficially resembles the septomarginal trabecula found in reptilian and mammalian hearts in that it spans the ventricle.
An olfactory receptor cells with apical microvilli.  The pear shaped soma is located in the apical region of the olfactory epithelium. They project to a single glomerulus, mdG5.
Vessel that drains aqueous humor from the eye.

comments: ZDB-PUB-140728-8 says the canal has lymphatic-like.

definition.val: A cell of a  hematopoietic lineage.
Interconnected lymph vasculature in the anterior portion of the head.
Superficial lymph vessel that is part of pharyngeal arch 3.   The vessel sprouts from the lateral facial lymph vessel.
Superficial lymph vessel that is part of pharyngeal arch 4.   The vessel sprouts from the lateral facial lymph vessel.
Superficial lymph vessel that is part of pharyngeal arch 5.   The vessel sprouts from the lateral facial lymph vessel.
Superficial lymph vessel that is part of pharyngeal arch 6.   The vessel sprouts from the lateral facial lymph vessel.
Lymphangiogenic sprout that  originates from the common cardinal vein and extends along the primary head sinus.

comments:
Although a facial lymphatic vessel has previously been described using lymphangiography in 5-week-old zebrafish (Yaniv et al., 2006), we are not sure whether
this previously described vessel is connected to, or derived from, the FL network that we have identified (ZDB-PUB-120529-43).

definition.val: Superfical lymph vessel part of the facial lymphatic network  that develops from the facial lymphatic sprout.  The lateral facial lymph vessel  runs toward the mandibular arch and tracks around the eye toward the jaw.
Facial lymphatic vessel that sprouts from the lateral facial lymph vessel.  The path lies  parallel to the posterior cerebral vein but is not in contact with a blood vessel.
Facial lymph vessel that sprouts from the lateral facial lymph vessel.  The path of the medial vessel runs ventromedially towards the ventral aorta.
Lymph vasculature surrounding the intestines.
Lymph vessel that follows the supraintestinal artery.

Pair of lymph vessel that follows the right subintestinal vein. At  6 dpf, the LR-IL appears to bifurcate and form two separate vessels that both run in close proximity to one another along the right sub intestinal vein. These vessels are difficult to distinguish from each other at 6dp.
Lymph vessels that are on the right side of the intestine.
Any of the tubular multi-tissue structures through which lymph moves in the body. These vessels collect and drain fluids and macromolecules from interstitial spaces throughout the animal.
Lymph vessel that extends along the left subintestinal vein.
Lymph vessels that are on the left side of the intestine.  These do not run as far posteriorly as the right intestinal lymphatics.
Lymph vessel on the left of the intestine that runs parallel to the upper right intestinal lymph vessel.  There does not appear to be an associated blood vessel.
Lymph vessel that connects lymph vessels within the intestinal lymphatic network.
Is a multi-tissue structure composed of dentine, enameloid and tooth pulp that develops from the dental organ.  These structures successively replace the primary  ceratobranchial teeth.

A cluster of neurons first visible in the embryonic diencephalon located in the dorsolateral brain just posterior to the posterior commissure,  that project growth cones ventrally along the posterior commissure to the anterior tegmentum where the PC intersects two longitudinal tracts, the tract of the postoptic commissure (TPOC) and the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF). Once at the intersection, nuc PC growth cones turn posteriorly onto the TPOC in the dorsal tegmentum and follow it to the hindbrain.

synonyms.val: nucleus of the tract of the posterior commissure

definition.val: Is a multi-tissue structure composed of dentine, enameloid and tooth pulp that develops from the dental organ. These are the first teeth to develop.
Ceratobranchial 5 tooth which is posterior to tooth 3V and anterior to tooth 5V in the ventral tooth row.  This tooth develops from the dental epithelium  at the position of  primary tooth 4V.
Ceratobranchial 5 tooth which is the posteriormost tooth in the ventral tooth row. This tooth develops from the dental epithelium  at the position of  primary tooth 5V.
Ceratobranchial 5 tooth which is posterior to tooth 2V and anterior to tooth 4V in the ventral tooth row. This tooth develops from the dental epithelium  at the position of  primary tooth 3V.
